title: Gate on fast-tier p99 and standard-tier p95
preview: Summary: This PR adds tier-aware SLO gating to perf-canary for the Workload-aware Batching Defaults launch. We now fail the perf-canary run if: (1) FAST tier p99 latency regresses beyond threshold (tail-latency protection gate), or (2) STANDARD tier p95 latency regresses beyond threshold (steady-state gate). The intent is to stop merges that silently violate the published tier SLOs while still allowing legitimate improvements to land with a controlled baseline refresh process. Motivation: As we move batching config from ad-hoc per-route knobs to three customer-facing tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient), we need CI guardrails that match what we’ll ship. Previously, canary gating was based on a single aggregate p95 and a single scenario per model. That missed fast-tier tail regressions (p99) and could overreact to noise in standard tier when concurrency ramps changed. What changed (high level): (1) Adds a tier_slo_gates.yaml config describing which percentile to gate on per tier, thresholds, and the scenario → tier mapping. (2) Extends the results parser to ingest tier labels from benchmark-lab output (scenario metadata), and compute per-tier regression deltas against the stored baseline. (3) Adds a baseline refresh workflow with explicit approvals, including a short “baseline justification” note that is stored next to the refreshed baseline file. (4) Adds a regression triage runbook tailored for tiered batching: how to distinguish scheduler regressions vs workload-mix drift, and what artifacts to attach to the Linear issue. Details: Gating rules: FAST tier: gate on p99 end-to-end latency, primary metric = request_latency_ms (includes queue + prefill + decode), scenario group = mixed_workload_streaming_shortprefill + mixed_workload_nonstreaming_shortprefill. Threshold default = +6% relative regression OR +25ms absolute (whichever triggers first) with a minimum sample size requirement. STANDARD tier: gate on p95 end-to-end latency, scenario group = mixed_workload_balanced + summarization_mediumprefill. Threshold default = +5% relative regression OR +35ms absolute. Note: COST-EFFICIENT tier is intentionally not a merge-blocking gate in this PR; we still compute and report it, but it only posts a warning annotation. This matches the staged rollout plan and avoids blocking on known variance for aggressive batching experiments. Noise controls: (a) sample-size gate: if we have < N requests for a tier/scenario, the gate is “insufficient data” and does not fail the run (but posts a warning). (b) warm-up exclusion: ignore first K requests in each scenario to reduce cold-start variance. (c) optional “max allowed stddev” heuristic (disabled by default) included behind a flag for future tuning. Baseline refresh process (new): We now store baselines under baselines/<model_family>/<hardware_class>/baseline.json and include a matching baselines/<...>/baseline.meta.txt with: timestamp, perf-canary run URL, benchmark-lab revision, and a one-paragraph human summary of why the refresh is safe (e.g., “intentional latency improvement due to scheduler tweak”). To refresh: run ./scripts/refresh_baseline.sh --model-family llama --hw h100 --scenario-set tiers --run-id <canary-run> then open a PR containing only baseline files + meta. That PR must have approvals from one Runtime reviewer and one SRE reviewer. Regression triage notes (new docs): Added docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md. Key guidance: verify the tier label in the artifact, check queue depth & batch-size distribution in the attached benchmark-lab plots, compare streaming vs non-streaming deltas (fast-tier p99 is usually driven by admission/wait budget), and tag the resulting Linear issue with runtime+slo-gate. Also includes “when to refresh baseline” vs “when to block merge and fix” decision table. Changed areas/files (high level): - config/tier_slo_gates.yaml: new canonical gating config - src/gating/tier_slo_gate.py: new tier-aware gate implementation - src/results/parser.py: scenario metadata → tier mapping + percentile extraction - src/cli.py: new flags for tier gating, warm-up exclusion - scripts/refresh_baseline.sh: baseline refresh helper + guardrails - docs/baseline_refresh.md: refresh procedure + required approvals - docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md: triage playbook - .github/workflows/perf-canary.yml: upload tier gate summary, annotate PRs Commit summary: 1) parser: ingest scenario metadata and emit tier breakdown 2) gating: implement fast p99 + standard p95 gates w/ thresholds 3) scripts/docs: add baseline refresh + triage docs 4) workflow: upload artifacts + PR annotations Validation: - Ran on 6 recent canary runs for top model families; no false failures after warm-up exclusion and sample-size checks - Verified that a known fast-tier p99 regression (synthetic +12% added wait budget) fails the gate - Verified that a known standard-tier improvement (scheduler prefill/decode split change) passes and baseline can be refreshed cleanly Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Add an optional gate for COST-EFFICIENT tier once variance is reduced (likely after we stabilize queue backpressure heuristics) - Integrate “reason codes” (profile selection telemetry) into the canary report when available from runtime 1.22 - Expand coverage to additional regions/hardware classes once Dedicated fleet baselines exist

title: Update release notes for runtime 1.22.0 (FP8/INT8 perf sprint)
preview: Summary: Finalize the runtime 1.22.0 release notes for the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint outcomes. This PR consolidates the kernel + scheduler changes that shipped behind flags, documents the rollout/canary plan and rollback triggers, and adds explicit guardrails/limitations discovered during the sprint (shape regimes, long-context caveats, and per-region canary learnings). It also aligns wording with Perf Transparency guidance so the same notes can be used internally (release channel) without accidentally making externally-unapproved benchmark claims. Context: This release packages the work from ENG-7422/7423/7424/7425/7426/7427/7430/7431 including (a) FP8 attention kernel integration behind flags, (b) INT8 matmul dispatch + shape guardrails and fallback behavior, (c) kernel auto-selection heuristics for H100/A100 with safe fallbacks, (d) scheduler knob defaults for mixed prefill/decode workloads, and (e) perf canary gating by model/region with improved metric tagging. What’s in the notes: Release highlights, flag names + default states, expected perf impact ranges (TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s) expressed as ranges and conditioned on workload and GPU SKU, known limitations/unsupported shapes, quality sign-off requirements for enabling quantized defaults, and operational verification checklist for SRE + oncall. No functional code changes beyond docs/changelog metadata.

title: Emit reason codes + tracing for workload-aware batching profile selection
preview: Summary: This PR adds explicit reason codes for workload-aware batching profile selection and wires them into structured metrics + tracing so we can answer “what profile did we pick and why?” during canary + rollout. Motivation: As we introduce SLO-tier defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient) we need explainability for operators and for our own regression triage. Today, selection is implicit in scheduler logs and hard to aggregate. This change emits a small, bounded set of reason codes and attaches them to a low-cardinality metric and to a trace span so that we can (a) break down latency/cost by effective profile and (b) understand which policy/constraint caused the selection without parsing logs. Scope: runtime scheduler decision instrumentation only (no policy logic changes, no knob changes). Reason codes are emitted at the final “effective profile” boundary after overrides + constraints have been resolved. Reason codes (v1): TIER_DEFAULT (selected tier default for model family/hw class); ROUTE_OVERRIDE (route policy explicitly selected a tier/profile); TENANT_OVERRIDE (Dedicated/tenant-level override forced); MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK (requested tier incompatible; fell back to nearest compatible profile); OVERLOAD_MODE (overload backpressure path forced conservative/alternate profile); SAFETY_GUARDRAIL (guardrail forced smaller batch / lower wait budget). Telemetry: 1) Counter: redwood.runtime.batching.profile_selection_total{tier,reason_code,model_family,hw_class,is_streaming} incremented once per request at admission, using model_family (not full model name) and hw_class (e.g., h100/a100/l4) to keep cardinality bounded. 2) Gauge/hist (existing) labels: plumb “effective_tier” into existing queue/latency attribution context (no new high-card tags). 3) Structured event (debug-only): scheduler emits a structured log payload with selected profile + reason_code + minimal inputs (prompt_len_bucket, concurrency_bucket) behind a runtime flag for targeted investigations. Tracing: Adds a span batching.profile_select under the request root span with attributes: effective_tier, reason_code, model_family, is_streaming, prompt_len_bucket. The span is created only when tracing is enabled and respects existing sampling; no new sampling policy introduced. API/compat: No public API changes. These signals are internal telemetry. Follow-ups: Metric tier labels/cardinality controls are being tightened in PR-28492; this PR intentionally avoids route_id / tenant_id labels. Dedicated override precedence is handled in PR-28503 (this PR just reports TENANT_OVERRIDE when that path is active). Testing: Added unit tests for reason code mapping across (a) pure tier default, (b) route override, (c) incompatibility fallback, (d) overload mode. Added a light integration test that asserts span attributes are present when tracing enabled. Rollout: Safe to land behind existing telemetry pipelines; the counter is additive and uses standard naming conventions. SRE note: initial dashboard panels can key off reason_code to validate expected selection distribution during canary (e.g., low MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK unless we’re missing profiles).

title: Fix race in rollout step transitions that can double-execute rollback
preview: Context: While wiring SLO-driven auto actions into the rollout controller, we observed occasional duplicate rollback execution in staging when the controller received concurrent signals (SLO evaluator tick + manual pause/rollback request, or duplicate queue deliveries). The symptom was two rollback executors attempting to revert the same rollout step, producing: (a) duplicate audit events, (b) traffic split “thrash” (two sequences of step-downs), and (c) occasional terminal state mismatch (rollout marked ROLLED_BACK while the step transition record was still in PROGRESS). Root cause: Step transitions were not guarded by a single-writer constraint. The controller’s state machine allowed two goroutines to pass the precondition checks ("step is eligible to transition") before either persisted the new step state, because step status and rollout version were read outside a transaction and the executor call was launched before we had a durable transition token. Fix: 1) Add a per-rollout transition lock implemented as a DB-backed compare-and-swap (CAS) on (rollout_id, step_id, transition_seq). We now acquire a transition lease row (rollout_step_transitions) using INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, and treat the resulting transition_id as the single durable token for the rest of the transition. 2) Make rollback execution idempotent by requiring an action_id (transition_id) and recording executor progress in rollout_action_executions with a UNIQUE(rollout_id, action_id, action_type). Re-entrancy becomes a no-op and returns the previously computed result. 3) Move state updates to a transaction that (a) verifies the expected step_state + rollout_generation, (b) writes transition record, (c) sets step_state=TRANSITIONING with transition_id, and only then enqueues executor work referencing transition_id. 4) Add explicit handling for retry/replay: if we see step_state=TRANSITIONING with an active transition_id, we return "already transitioning" and do not schedule a second executor job. 5) Add targeted metrics + logs: rollout_step_transition_conflict_total, rollout_action_idempotent_replay_total, and structured log fields rollout_id/step_id/transition_id for correlation with the new audit stream. Testing: Added unit tests for CAS contention, executor idempotency, and transition replay; added a staging simulation test that sends concurrent "auto-rollback" and "manual rollback" requests and asserts only one transition + one executor run occurs. Rollout impact: This is a correctness fix that reduces the probability of traffic split oscillation and duplicate rollback actions under load. No API changes. References: Found while testing auto-rollback SLO integration; ties directly to staging findings from SUP-19422-like scenario and the controller hook work in pr-28411/pr-28444.

title: E2E (staging): simulate SLO breaches and verify auto-pause/rollback + audit/Console surfaces
preview:
What
Adds end-to-end coverage in staging for the new auto-mitigation path (SLO breach -> controller decision -> action execution -> audit trail -> Console surfaces). These tests are intended to gate staged enablement and catch regressions in integration points (monitor adapter, policy engine, controller state machine, audit event emission, Console API/UI).

Why
We’ve had multiple staging runs where unit/integration tests passed but the real system failed to auto-mitigate due to timing/labeling issues (monitor staleness, wrong rollout scope labels, eventual consistency between controller and Console read model). This PR adds deterministic(ish) staging E2E that:
- injects synthetic latency + error signals for a specific rollout scope (route/model_version/region/tier)
- waits for SLO evaluator to observe a breach (fast window) with minimum sample gating
- asserts controller pauses at the correct step AND can roll back when configured
- validates audit events and Console API surfaces match expected schema/fields

Scope / approach
- Uses the staging-only signal injector endpoint behind RBAC + allowlist (existing internal tooling). The injector emits metric series matching the rollout-scoped labels used by the SLO toolkit burn-rate endpoint.
- Exercises both actions:
  1) auto-pause (default MVP behavior) on latency burn
  2) auto-rollback (policy override) on sustained 5xx burn
- Validates that suppression is respected when the rollout has an active suppression window (smoke only; full suppression matrix covered elsewhere).

Test flow (high level)
1) Create a test rollout via rollouts API for the canary route `chat.completions` with model baseline -> candidate and a deterministic rollout_id prefix `e2e-ar-<ts>`.
2) Apply a rollout policy template `staging_e2e_auto_rollback` (short windows + lowered sample size thresholds, but still above safe minimums) scoped to the rollout route/model/region.
3) Begin rollout and advance to the first traffic-shift step (controller driven).
4) Inject synthetic signals:
   - Latency case: emit p95 latency series above threshold (fast burn) for candidate slice only.
   - Error case: emit 5xx error rate series above threshold for candidate slice only.
5) Poll controller state until an auto decision is recorded (bounded wait w/ jitter) and assert:
   - decision outcome == PAUSE or ROLLBACK as expected
   - decision includes monitor_ids, policy_version, and a metrics snapshot reference
   - controller step state transitions are monotonic (no double-execution)
6) Verify audit trail:
   - audit event exists in event stream (type `rollout.auto_action`)
   - payload fields match ADR-0128-required keys (rollout_id, action, reason, monitor timestamps, suppression flag)
7) Verify Console surfaces:
   - Console backend endpoint `/v1/rollouts/{id}/auto-actions` returns the decision
   - Console timeline read model contains the entry with stable ordering
   - (Playwright) basic UI check: event renders with “Why did we pause/rollback?” panel and links are non-empty (dashboard link uses a staging host).

Reliability / flake resistance
- All waits are bounded (max 8m) and include backoff + jitter; we fail fast on explicit staleness signals (`monitor_missing`, `monitor_stale`).
- Injector emits for a fixed duration and then stops; test cleans up created rollouts and policies in an `afterAll` hook.
- Added explicit propagation guard: we wait for the SLO toolkit to report non-zero sample size for the rollout scope before asserting breach.

Security
- Staging injector requires `rollouts.e2e.write` and is only enabled in staging; tests run under the CI service account that already has staging E2E privileges.
- Test data uses synthetic customer tier `internal` and does not touch prod.

Rollout / enablement
- Introduces a new CI job `e2e-staging-auto-rollback` that runs nightly and on PRs labeled `e2e` (required only for rollouts-related paths). For this PR, it’s set as required for merge in `redwood/rollouts` CODEOWNERS paths.

Notes
- This PR intentionally uses shorter evaluation windows (1m/5m) in staging policies to keep runtime reasonable. These are only used for E2E templates; production defaults remain unchanged.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4844
- Related PRs: pr-28411, pr-28435, pr-28444, pr-28458, pr-28463, pr-28471

   - Console timeline read model contains the entry with stable ordering
   - (Playwright) basic UI check: event renders with “Why did we pause/rollback?” panel and links are non-empty (dashboard link uses a staging host).

Reliability / flake resistance
- All waits are bounded (max 8m) and include backoff + jitter; we fail fast on explicit staleness signals (`monitor_missing`, `monitor_stale`).
- Injector emits for a fixed duration and then stops; test cleans up created rollouts and policies in an `afterAll` hook.
- Added explicit propagation guard: we wait for the SLO toolkit to report non-zero sample size for the rollout scope before asserting breach.

Security
- Staging injector requires `rollouts.e2e.write` and is only enabled in staging; tests run under the CI service account that already has staging E2E privileges.
- Test data uses synthetic customer tier `internal` and does not touch prod.

Rollout / enablement
- Introduces a new CI job `e2e-staging-auto-rollback` that runs nightly and on PRs labeled `e2e` (required only for rollouts-related paths). For this PR, it’s set as required for merge in `redwood/rollouts` CODEOWNERS paths.

Notes
- This PR intentionally uses shorter evaluation windows (1m/5m) in staging policies to keep runtime reasonable. These are only used for E2E templates; production defaults remain unchanged.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4844
- Related PRs: pr-28411, pr-28435, pr-28444, pr-28458, pr-28463, pr-28471

title: Post rollout auto-rollback events to Slack (concise notification + links)
preview: Summary: incident-bot now posts a Slack notification when the rollout controller triggers an automatic safety action due to an SLO breach (auto_rollback, auto_pause, or rollback_suppressed). The message is intentionally short, includes the rollout_id and scope, and provides deep links to (1) the rollout in Console, (2) the rollout SLO dashboard, and (3) the audit event details. Motivation: during the 2026-02 simulations and recent customer escalations, oncall often learned about auto-mitigations only after secondary alerts or customer reports; this closes the loop and reduces time-to-context while keeping noise bounded. Event source: consumes the new structured audit event emitted to the event stream (redwood PR-28435) with event_type=rollout.auto_action.*. The handler maps event subtypes to Slack severity and routing without paging by default (notify-only) to avoid paging storms. Message format (example): "[AUTO-ROLLBACK] rollout=rol_01HT… scope=route:/v1/chat model=llama3.1-70b-v3 region=us-east tier=dedicated reason=p99_latency_burnrate_fast" followed by links: Console rollout, Grafana dashboard, Audit event (internal). Guardrails: (a) dedupe by (rollout_id, action, step_id) for 10 minutes, (b) drop messages if required links can’t be constructed (still increments a metric), (c) redacts customer identifiers (uses customer_tier + optional org_id hash only), (d) rate limits per channel to 1 msg/sec with burst=5. Routing: auto_rollback -> #eng-oncall (notify) + #eng-sre (notify); auto_pause -> #eng-platform (notify); rollback_suppressed -> thread reply on the most recent auto action message if present, otherwise #eng-sre (notify). Configuration: adds rollout_auto_actions.enabled (default false in prod; true in staging), rollout_auto_actions.channels overrides, and a link template block for Console/Grafana base URLs per environment. Observability: emits incident_bot_slack_posts_total{type=rollout_auto_action,outcome=sent|dropped|rate_limited|deduped} and incident_bot_rollout_auto_action_events_total{action=rollback|pause|suppressed}. Testing: unit tests cover payload parsing, formatting, dedupe, and redaction; an integration test uses a fixture event to validate generated Slack blocks; updated run instructions in README. Rollout plan: staging enablement first (paired with redwood PR-28490), then prod behind flag once dashboards/alerts are confirmed (observability-pack PR-913).

title: Add Cost Model API Grafana dashboards (latency/errors, schema version dist, drift)
preview: Summary: Adds a Grafana folder + dashboards for the Cost Model API so Console/Optimize rollouts have a single place to monitor health, adoption, and correctness drift. Includes panels for request volume, p50/p95/p99 latency, error-rate by code, schema version distribution, request-bounds rejections, cache hit/miss (client-side + service memoization), and a first pass of drift metrics (predicted vs observed) sourced from the scheduled drift job. Motivation: During early Console integration testing we saw two recurring issues: (1) latency spikes due to cardinality blowups on hot paths and (2) drift alerts firing due to telemetry lag vs genuine mismatch. This PR standardizes the dashboards and bakes in guardrail signals (telemetry freshness, missing inputs) so oncall can quickly triage. Related: redwood/pr-28457 (service endpoints), redwood/pr-28490 (drift monitoring job), redwood/pr-28495 (what-if guardrails), SUP-31905 (latency spike report). What’s included (dashboards): 1) "Cost Model API / Overview" (high-level SLO views): RPS, concurrency, saturation, p50/p95/p99 latency, error budget burn (5m/1h/6h), top tenants (bounded), and a "schema negotiation" panel showing unknown/unsupported versions returned to clients. 2) "Cost Model API / Errors & Bounds" (debug): error rate split by status, error_code, and stage; panels for bound rejections (e.g., breakdown_group_by too high-cardinality), request payload size buckets, and timeouts by caller (console-backend vs optimize-service). 3) "Cost Model API / Schema Version Adoption": distribution of requested schema_version vs served schema_version (incl. fallback), percent of clients sending optional fields for cache/batching/quantization; includes a heatmap of version by environment. 4) "Cost Model / Drift": predicted_cost_usd vs observed_cost_usd (daily + hourly), drift pct, confidence bands where available, and triage panels: telemetry lag (ingestion watermark age), missing-runtime-input rate, and "prefill/decode split present" ratio. Alerts: Adds two alert rules (behind the existing oncall routing policy): (a) p95 latency > 300ms for 10m (scoped to prod + excluding canary envs), (b) drift_pct > 5% for 6h AND telemetry_lag < 15m (to reduce false positives during pipeline delays). Notes: Drift is expected to be noisy early; the dashboard intentionally surfaces "telemetry freshness" next to drift so oncall can distinguish pipeline lag from real regression. Implementation details: Dashboards live under grafana/dashboards/cost-model-api/*.jsonnet and are rendered to JSON in CI. Metrics consumed assume the cost-model service exports: cost_model_api_request_duration_seconds (histogram), cost_model_api_requests_total (by status, error_code, caller, env), cost_model_api_schema_version_served_total, and drift job exports: cost_model_drift_ratio, cost_model_drift_pct, cost_model_observed_cost_usd, cost_model_predicted_cost_usd, telemetry_watermark_age_seconds. Rollout: Once merged, SRE will deploy the updated observability pack on the next release train; Console rollout doc will link to these dashboards. Screenshots: Added to the internal docs PR separately (redwood-docs/pr-1187) to avoid bloating this repo.

title: Telemetry: add normalized batch efficiency + queue-time metrics for Cost Model inputs
preview:
Summary
This PR adds and normalizes batch efficiency and queue-time metrics in the telemetry pipeline so Cost Model API v1 can consume stable, low-cardinality, versioned inputs across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Motivation / Context
The Standard Cost Model project requires consistent inputs for: (1) batch efficiency (how effectively requests share a batch over time) and (2) queueing delay (scheduler wait) to accurately attribute request cost and explain cost deltas in Console/Optimize. Today, runtime emits a mix of per-request and per-batch stats with inconsistent naming/units, and the ingestion layer normalizes only a subset. This creates drift across billing hooks vs Console breakdowns and makes Optimize what-if projections hard to ground.

What’s included (high level)
- Adds a canonical set of metrics (names + units + tags) for batch efficiency and queue-time.
- Normalizes legacy metric names from runtime exporters into the canonical contract.
- Enforces bounded-cardinality tag policies (drops request_id/pod_id; whitelists stable dimensions).
- Adds data-quality checks and dashboards-facing metadata (histogram bucket guidance, required/optional tags).
- Adds a lightweight “metric contract” test to prevent accidental rename/unit regressions.

Canonical metrics added
1) redwood.runtime.scheduler.queue_time_ms (histogram)
   - Definition: time from request admission to assignment into an execution batch.
   - Unit: milliseconds.
   - Tags (whitelist): deployment_mode, region, model_id, route, gpu_sku, scheduler_policy, quant_profile_id.
   - Notes: excludes client/network time; aligns with runtime scheduler timestamps.

2) redwood.runtime.batch.efficiency_ratio (gauge)
   - Definition: effective_batch_tokens / (max_batch_tokens_possible) over a fixed window (default 10s) within a scheduler shard.
   - Range: [0.0, 1.0].
   - Tags: deployment_mode, region, model_id, gpu_sku, scheduler_policy, quant_profile_id.
   - Notes: windowed to avoid per-request cardinality; intended as a workload-context signal, not a billing primitive by itself.

3) redwood.runtime.batch.active_size (histogram)
   - Definition: number of concurrent sequences in the active batch at dispatch time (per-dispatch sampled).
   - Unit: count.
   - Tags: deployment_mode, region, model_id, gpu_sku, scheduler_policy.

4) redwood.runtime.batch.dispatch_interval_ms (histogram)
   - Definition: time between batch dispatch events within a shard (helps interpret “efficiency” vs latency).
   - Unit: milliseconds.

Legacy normalization / mapping
- runtime.scheduler.queue_ms -> redwood.runtime.scheduler.queue_time_ms
- runtime.batch.utilization -> redwood.runtime.batch.efficiency_ratio
- runtime.batch.size -> redwood.runtime.batch.active_size
Mappings are implemented in telemetry-ingestion so old exporters remain compatible during rollout. Where units differed, we apply explicit conversion at ingestion (ms vs ns).

Cardinality and tagging policy
To keep the Cost Model API from inheriting high-cardinality telemetry:
- Hard drop tags: request_id, trace_id, pod, node, gpu_uuid.
- Allowlist only stable dims listed above.
- route tag is normalized to “route_template” (e.g., /v1/chat/completions) rather than concrete URL.
- tenant_id is explicitly NOT included for these metrics; per-tenant cost attribution should rely on token metering streams and request-level metering events, not scheduler histograms.

Implementation details
- Adds new runtime-metric schema entries in telemetry-contracts and updates the ingestion transform rules.
- Emits histograms with fixed buckets (per observability guidance) to keep TSDB cost controlled.
- Adds a conformance test that verifies: metric name, type (gauge/histogram), unit annotation, and tag allowlist.

Changed areas / files (representative)
- telemetry/contracts/runtime_metrics.yaml
- telemetry/ingestion/transforms/runtime_metric_normalization.ts
- telemetry/ingestion/policies/tag_allowlist.ts
- telemetry/tests/metric_contracts.test.ts
- docs/internal/telemetry/metric-catalog.md

Commits (squash summary)
- Add canonical runtime scheduler queue-time metric + normalization
- Introduce batch efficiency ratio + active batch size histograms
- Enforce tag allowlist + drop high-cardinality tags
- Add metric contract tests and documentation

Validation
- Backfilled sample from staging for 24h and compared distributions vs existing runtime dashboards.
- Verified queue_time_ms correlates with known scheduler pressure events.
- Confirmed no per-tenant cardinality increase in TSDB.

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (this PR): ingestion normalization + contract tests; exporters can remain unchanged.
- Phase 2 (follow-up): update runtime exporter names to canonical and delete legacy mappings after 2 release cycles.

Related work
- ENG-4823 Telemetry input contracts for cost model
- ENG-4828 Runtime instrumentation for KV cache + batch efficiency
- Cost Model API v1 will consume these as workload-context inputs (not billing record of truth).

Review notes / discussion
- Thomas Baird requested removing route from two metrics due to potential cardinality; we kept route only on queue_time_ms and normalized it to route_template (no query params). Added a guard in tag_allowlist to strip unmatched routes.
- Omar El-Sayed asked for clarification on whether efficiency_ratio should be per-stage (prefill vs decode). We’re explicitly defining it as scheduler-level dispatch efficiency (stage-agnostic) for v1; stage split remains a runtime-side metric used elsewhere.
- Rafael Mendes requested explicit histogram buckets and a limit on unique tag combinations per shard; added fixed buckets and a per-minute “unique series” warning log (non-blocking) in ingestion.

CI
- build (monorepo): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- telemetry-contracts conformance: pass
- staging replay (sample dataset): pass

Notes for Cost Model consumers
- Use efficiency_ratio and dispatch_interval_ms as context inputs for interpreting cost variance (batching conditions), not as direct multipliers without calibration.
- queue_time_ms is safe for SLO overlays in Console; it excludes network/client time by definition.

Follow-ups
- Remove legacy metric name support after runtime exporter rollout is complete (tracked in ENG-4828).
- Add a small “metric freshness” panel to Cost Model drift dashboards to detect telemetry lag (tracked in ENG-4827).

title: Perf canary: gate new kernels by model + region with auto-rollback on threshold breach
preview:
Motivation: We need to safely roll out FP8/INT8 kernel paths without globally flipping flags. During the sprint we saw model- and region-specific perf deltas (plus noisy samples in low-traffic regions) that make coarse-grained gating too risky. This PR adds per-(model, region) cohort gating for kernel modes, with automatic rollback when TTFT/TPOT breach configured thresholds over a stable sample window.

What this changes (high level): 1) Adds a cohort-aware gate that evaluates perf canary metrics keyed by {model_id, region, gpu_sku, kernel_mode}. 2) Introduces a config-driven rollout plan format that allows staged enablement by cohort (e.g., start with us-east for top-2 models on H100, then expand). 3) Implements auto-rollback: if a cohort breaches threshold for N consecutive evaluation windows (with minimum sample constraints), the controller flips the cohort back to baseline and emits a structured event + page annotation. 4) Adds safety valves: manual override lock, per-cohort cooldown, and an allowlist for cohorts that must never be toggled automatically (Dedicated pools / protected regions).

Implementation details: - New module `runtime/perf_canary/cohort_gate.{cc,h}` that wraps existing threshold evaluation and adds cohort dimensioning + state machine (pending -> enabled -> monitoring -> rollback). - Cohort definitions loaded from dynamic config (`perf_canary/kernel_rollout_plan.yaml`) and optionally patched at runtime via config service (same path we use for feature flags). - Threshold profiles reuse the quantized-kernel profiles introduced in perf-canary/pr-611, but apply them per cohort. We require: min_requests >= 600 AND min_unique_sessions >= 60 per 30m window (defaults; override per model) to reduce small-sample false positives (ties to SUP-31881). - Auto-rollback sets `kernel_mode=baseline` for the cohort and posts an event to `#eng-sre` + adds an annotation tag `auto_rollback=true` with the computed deltas and window stats.

Config example: rollout plan snippet: `cohorts: - name: h100-us-east-top_models stage: 0 match: { regions: [us-east], gpu_skus: [h100], models: ["rw-llama3-70b", "rw-mixtral-8x7b"] } enable: { kernel_mode: fp8, flags: ["runtime.kernels.fp8_attention"] } thresholds: { profile: fp8_int8_default, max_p95_ttft_regression_pct: 7, max_p95_tpot_regression_pct: 5 } rollback: { consecutive_windows: 2, cooldown_minutes: 180 }`

Rollout/ops: - Default behavior is NO-OP unless a rollout plan explicitly enables cohorts. - Manual override: `perf_canary.cohort_gate.lock=true` disables any automated flips (useful during incidents). - Rollback trigger is conservative (requires consecutive windows + min samples), and we log the computed deltas with the baseline selector (baseline cohort is same model/region/gpu_sku with kernel_mode=baseline).

Testing: - Unit tests cover: cohort matching, baseline selection, threshold evaluation w/ min sample gating, state transitions, cooldown, and override lock. - Added an integration-style test using recorded metric fixtures to validate end-to-end auto-rollback behavior. - Ran locally against staging metrics (dry-run mode) and verified no config churn when cohorts are not enabled.

Notes / follow-ups: - This PR assumes metrics are tagged with model/gpu/kernel_mode; for older runtime builds, kernel_mode may be missing. In that case the gate will skip evaluation and emit a warning (we still recommend rolling out pr-19934 to fully support the dimension). - This PR does not change threshold tuning itself; it only scopes evaluation and action per cohort.

Checklist: - [x] Linked Linear (ENG-7426) - [x] Added unit tests for gate/state machine - [x] Added config docs + example rollout plan - [x] Added structured rollback event payload and Grafana annotation tags - [x] Verified safe default (disabled unless configured) - [x] Runbook updates: see confluence runbook "perf-canary-rollout-for-new-kernels" (follow-up PR will add screenshots to the doc)

Risk: Medium. This is control logic that can flip runtime behavior; mitigated by default-off, override lock, cooldown, and conservative sample requirements.

During the sprint we saw model- and region-specific perf deltas (plus noisy samples in low-traffic regions) that make coarse-grained gating too risky. This PR adds per-(model, region) cohort gating for kernel modes, with automatic rollback when TTFT/TPOT breach configured thresholds over a stable sample window.

What this changes (high level): 1) Adds a cohort-aware gate that evaluates perf canary metrics keyed by {model_id, region, gpu_sku, kernel_mode}. 2) Introduces a config-driven rollout plan format that allows staged enablement by cohort (e.g., start with us-east for top-2 models on H100, then expand). 3) Implements auto-rollback: if a cohort breaches threshold for N consecutive evaluation windows (with minimum sample constraints), the controller flips the cohort back to baseline and emits a structured event + page annotation. 4) Adds safety valves: manual override lock, per-cohort cooldown, and an allowlist for cohorts that must never be toggled automatically (Dedicated pools / protected regions).

Implementation details: - New module `runtime/perf_canary/cohort_gate.{cc,h}` that wraps existing threshold evaluation and adds cohort dimensioning + state machine (pending -> enabled -> monitoring -> rollback). - Cohort definitions loaded from dynamic config (`perf_canary/kernel_rollout_plan.yaml`) and optionally patched at runtime via config service (same path we use for feature flags). - Threshold profiles reuse the quantized-kernel profiles introduced in perf-canary/pr-611, but apply them per cohort. We require: min_requests >= 600 AND min_unique_sessions >= 60 per 30m window (defaults; override per model) to reduce small-sample false positives (ties to SUP-31881). - Auto-rollback sets `kernel_mode=baseline` for the cohort and posts an event to `#eng-sre` + adds an annotation tag `auto_rollback=true` with the computed deltas and window stats.

Config example: rollout plan snippet: `cohorts: - name: h100-us-east-top_models stage: 0 match: { regions: [us-east], gpu_skus: [h100], models: ["rw-llama3-70b", "rw-mixtral-8x7b"] } enable: { kernel_mode: fp8, flags: ["runtime.kernels.fp8_attention"] } thresholds: { profile: fp8_int8_default, max_p95_ttft_regression_pct: 7, max_p95_tpot_regression_pct: 5 } rollback: { consecutive_windows: 2, cooldown_minutes: 180 }`

Rollout/ops: - Default behavior is NO-OP unless a rollout plan explicitly enables cohorts. - Manual override: `perf_canary.cohort_gate.lock=true` disables any automated flips (useful during incidents). - Rollback trigger is conservative (requires consecutive windows + min samples), and we log the computed deltas with the baseline selector (baseline cohort is same model/region/gpu_sku with kernel_mode=baseline).

Testing: - Unit tests cover: cohort matching, baseline selection, threshold evaluation w/ min sample gating, state transitions, cooldown, and override lock. - Added an integration-style test using recorded metric fixtures to validate end-to-end auto-rollback behavior. - Ran locally against staging metrics (dry-run mode) and verified no config churn when cohorts are not enabled.

Notes / follow-ups: - This PR assumes metrics are tagged with model/gpu/kernel_mode; for older runtime builds, kernel_mode may be missing. In that case the gate will skip evaluation and emit a warning (we still recommend rolling out pr-19934 to fully support the dimension). - This PR does not change threshold tuning itself; it only scopes evaluation and action per cohort.

Checklist: - [x] Linked Linear (ENG-7426) - [x] Added unit tests for gate/state machine - [x] Added config docs + example rollout plan - [x] Added structured rollback event payload and Grafana annotation tags - [x] Verified safe default (disabled unless configured) - [x] Runbook updates: see confluence runbook "perf-canary-rollout-for-new-kernels" (follow-up PR will add screenshots to the doc)

Risk: Medium. This is control logic that can flip runtime behavior; mitigated by default-off, override lock, cooldown, and conservative sample requirements.

title: hotfix(runtime): disable FP8 attention kernel for small-q / mid-kv shape regime (canary TTFT regression)
preview:
Context: Perf canary flagged a p95 TTFT regression after enabling the FP8 attention kernel in eu-west for a subset of traffic. The regression is concentrated in a specific shape regime where the FP8 attention kernel launch/epilogue overhead dominates (small q_len decode steps + mid-range kv_len), leading to worse end-to-end TTFT despite kernel-level wins in other regimes.

This PR is a safety hotfix for 1.22.0: add a dispatch guard that disables FP8 attention for the known-bad shape regime and falls back to the baseline (BF16/FP16) attention kernel path. This is intended to unblock canary re-enable for the rest of the population while we root-cause and optimize the FP8 kernel for this regime.

Regime disabled (attention-only):
- q_len <= 16 (decode micro-steps / tool-calling structured-output workloads)
- 768 <= kv_len <= 3072 (mid-context; observed worst around ~2k)
- head_dim == 128 (H100/A100 common config)
- batch_size <= 4 (high-concurrency, low-batch scenarios)

Notes:
- Guard is applied only to the FP8 attention kernel dispatch; FP8 matmul paths remain unchanged.
- Adds a structured counter + log tag so we can quantify how often we hit the fallback (dimensioned by model/gpu/region).

Impact:
- Expected: removes the canary regression for the affected cohort; small throughput decrease for that narrow regime, but net win vs leaving FP8 enabled.
- No API surface changes.

Validation:
- Reproduced regression with canary workload replay (top-hosted benchmark suite v3, decode-heavy mix) on H100.
- Verified correctness parity via smoke prompts (no output deltas beyond normal sampling variance).
- Ran targeted A/B on the failing cases: TTFT returns to baseline once the guard forces fallback.

Follow-ups:
- ENG-7429: investigate kernel launch config for small-q decode + kv_len ~2k (likely persistent tile sizing / epilogue fusion).
- ENG-7426: update perf-canary threshold profile to separately track this regime as a dedicated dimension.

Links:
- Jira: SUP-31844 (customer-reported p95 latency regression correlated with FP8 enablement)
- Slack: canary rollback thread (eng-sre) + kernel regression thread (eng-runtime)

Checklist:
- [x] Guarded dispatch with explicit shape predicate + unit tests
- [x] Fallback is baseline attention kernel (no recursion / no double-dispatch)
- [x] Added metric for fallback hit-rate
- [x] Confirmed no perf regression outside the guarded regime
- [x] Ready for release/1.22.0 cherry-pick (this PR targets release directly)

title: Cost Model API: add policy guardrails for what-if projections (bounds + allowed ranges)
preview: Context: The new Cost Model what-if endpoint is intentionally flexible (batching/caching/quantization knobs) and will be called by Optimize (interactive) and potentially Console (hot paths). In early staging tests and after SUP-31877 triage, we saw two failure modes: (1) callers passing extreme parameter values (e.g., batch size 4096, cache warm-rate > 1.0) leading to unrealistic savings outputs, and (2) expensive computations when callers submit high-cardinality workload summaries (large histograms / too many route keys) that can blow our latency budget and increase tail p95s. This PR adds explicit policy guardrails at the API boundary so the service is safe-by-default, deterministic, and bounded. Summary of changes: (A) Introduce a centralized PolicyGuardrails layer for what-if requests that validates and clamps input ranges (where clamping is explicitly allowed) and rejects invalid/unsafe requests with clear error codes. (B) Enforce request bounds for cardinality: max number of workload segments, max histogram buckets, max distinct route/model keys, and max scenario count per request. (C) Add a compute budget heuristic: estimate worst-case complexity from the request and reject if above threshold (protects CPU and avoids unbounded fanout to model catalog/telemetry lookups). (D) Add an allowlist for parameterized what-if dimensions in v1 (batching, prefix-cache, kv-cache, quant profile change) and explicitly reject unknown/experimental dimensions unless the caller is in an internal allowlist (feature flag + service-to-service identity). (E) Standardize error responses for guardrail failures: HTTP 400 with error_code=CM_GUARDRAIL_* for caller-correctable issues; HTTP 403 for forbidden dimensions/tenancy; HTTP 429 for rate-limit triggers; and a consistent message + remediation hints so Optimize can surface actionable UI text. Guardrails (v1): - scenario_count: max 10 scenarios per request; - workload_segments: max 25 segments (Optimize currently uses <= 8); - histogram buckets: max 32 buckets for seq_len distribution and max 32 for output_len distribution; - route/model keys: max 50 distinct keys; - batch_size_target: [1, 256] (reject outside); - microbatch_size_target: [1, 64]; - cache_hit_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; no clamping); - cache_warm_rate: [0.0, 1.0] (reject outside; note: renamed internally to warm_rate to avoid ambiguity); - quantization_profile_id: must exist in model catalog and must be compatible with base model; - overhead_allocation_policy: must be one of {request_attributable_only, blended_cluster_overhead}; - max_estimated_compute_units: 1.0 (internal scale); reject above threshold. Observability: adds metrics cost_model.whatif.guardrail_reject_total{reason=...} and cost_model.whatif.request_shape{segments=...,buckets=...,scenarios=...} to support tuning. Also adds structured log fields guardrail_reason and request_shape_summary for incident triage. Tests: unit tests for validation + error mapping; golden tests ensuring deterministic output and stable error codes; load-test fixture ensuring guardrails prevent worst-case request shapes from entering compute. Notes: This PR intentionally rejects requests rather than silently clamping in most cases, to avoid producing misleading ROI estimates. The only clamping performed is for missing optional fields that have safe defaults (e.g., absent cache_hit_rate defaults to observed baseline). Follow-ups: (1) coordinate with Optimize to align UI input widgets with these ranges and surface error_code messages; (2) document guardrails in internal docs; (3) revisit limits after we collect two weeks of production telemetry on request shapes and latency.

title: Add FP8/INT8 benchmark + rollout Grafana dashboards and canary alerting
preview:
## Context
Serving Runtime is running an FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking + staged rollout sprint. SRE/Runtime asked for a single place to monitor benchmark runs (offline) and the perf-canary rollout (online) with consistent dimensions.

This PR adds a small dashboard pack + alert rules in `observability-pack`:
- Grafana dashboards for benchmark runs and rollout monitoring
- Prometheus recording rules to normalize TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s aggregations and reduce query cost
- Alert rules intended for perf canary gating (and incident-style triage)

## What’s included
### 1) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Benchmark Runs**
Folder: `Serving Runtime / Performance`
Primary purpose: benchmark execution monitoring + sanity checks while running the matrix.
Panels:
- TTFT (ms) p50/p95 by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8), `region`
- TPOT (ms/token) p50/p95 (decode-heavy visibility)
- Tokens/s (effective) and requests/s
- Error rate + error codes (runtime error class breakdown)
- GPU util (%), SM active (% if exported), GPU memory used/total, OOM counters
- Scheduler breakdown panels (if present): prefill vs decode time share, queue depth p95

Benchmark-specific UX:
- Requires `bench_run_id` label when available; otherwise filters by `job` + `git_sha`.
- Adds an annotation query for rollout toggles (if `kernel_mode` flag events are shipped; optional).

### 2) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Rollout / Perf Canary**
Primary purpose: compare canary cohort vs baseline cohort during staged enablement.
Panels:
- Canary vs baseline overlays for TTFT p95, TPOT p95, tokens/s p50 (time-aligned)
- Heatmap panels (TTFT buckets) to catch tail regressions masked by p95
- Slice-by tables (top regressions) grouped by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`
- Error budget quick-view: error rate, timeout rate, fallback rate (where emitted)
- GPU saturation + memory headroom (to detect scheduler/packing changes masquerading as kernel wins)

### 3) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – GPU Health (Perf Context)**
Primary purpose: quickly rule out “it’s the fleet” during noisy runs.
Panels:
- GPU clocks (when exported), thermal throttle counters (if present), GPU util and mem
- Node-level saturation and queue depth correlation
- Error spikes correlated with GPU OOM / CUDA errors

## Metrics and dimensions
This PR assumes the runtime metrics are tagged with (at minimum):
- `model`
- `gpu_sku`
- `region`
- `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8)

Where available, dashboards also use:
- `cohort` (baseline|canary) for perf-canary comparisons
- `prefill_decode` (prefill|decode) for scheduler splits

If any of these labels are missing, dashboards will still render but lose key filtering/overlays.

## Alerting (perf canary)
New alerts are scoped to the perf-canary use case (fast detection + low false positives). Summary:
- **PerfCanaryTTFTP95Regression**: TTFT p95 regression >= 8% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTPOTP95Regression**: TPOT p95 regression >= 6% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTokensPerSecDrop**: tokens/s p50 drop >= 7% vs baseline for 15m.
- **PerfCanaryErrorRateSpike**: error rate > 0.5% for 5m (or +3x vs baseline where comparable).
- **PerfCanaryOOMSpike**: GPU OOM counter increase above threshold.

Noise suppression:
- Recording rules compute rolling rates and quantiles on fixed windows.
- All perf-regression alerts include a `min_requests` gate to avoid small-sample false positives (feedback from #eng-sre).

Routing:
- Alerts default to `#eng-sre` / `#eng-runtime` (per existing alertmanager routing for the folder). No paging is configured by default; SRE can promote to paging per rollout.

## How to test
- `make grafana-render` (renders jsonnet → dashboard JSON)
- `make grafana-lint` (format + schema checks)
- `promtool test rules prometheus/tests/fp8_int8_alerts_test.yaml`

## Rollout / compatibility notes
- No changes to metric emission in this repo.
- If `kernel_mode` isn’t present yet in a given cluster, tables/overlays will show “(missing)” and queries will collapse across modes.
- The canary dashboard expects `cohort` labels if perf-canary is emitting them; otherwise use the `region` + `kernel_mode` filters.

## Screenshots
Attached in PR artifacts: benchmark overview (TTFT/TPOT), canary overlay, and slice-by table examples.

## Review notes / decision points
- Threshold defaults are intentionally conservative. Rafael requested we start with non-paging alerts and tighten once we see real noise levels.
- Marcus requested scheduler queue depth + prefill/decode split panels, included in this revision (panels gracefully hide if missing).

## Related
- Linear: ENG-7427 (dashboards), ENG-7426 (canary thresholds)
- Runtime PRs: metrics tagging for model/gpu/mode is tracked separately; this pack assumes those labels exist.

---
### Timeline
- 2026-02-13: Initial dashboard scaffolding + folder wiring
- 2026-02-16: Added recording rules + first alert pass
- 2026-02-18: Noise suppression + min-sample gating; added scheduler split panels
- 2026-02-19: Final review, promtool tests, merged

title: Optimize scheduler knob defaults for mixed prefill/decode workloads
preview:
Title: Optimize scheduler knob defaults for mixed prefill/decode workloads

Context
During the FP8/INT8 benchmarking sprint we saw several cases where microbench wins (kernel runtime) did not translate cleanly to end-to-end improvements in the hosted runtime under mixed workloads (concurrent requests with a realistic prefill/decode mix). The main failure mode was scheduler-induced head-of-line effects: decode-heavy phases could keep the GPU busy, but TTFT (and TTFT p95 in particular) regressed because prefill admission and batching windows were tuned for "throughput-first" steady-state rather than mixed interactive traffic.

This PR updates the default scheduler knob set for the "mixed" workload profile to better realize kernel wins without paying a TTFT tail penalty. Changes were validated against the v3 top-hosted benchmark suite and compared to baseline scheduler defaults.

What changed (high level)
1) Batching window defaults (mixed profile)
- Reduce default batching_window_ms in the mixed profile (and enforce tighter min/max bounds) to reduce time spent accumulating batches when there is steady decode pressure.
- Add small jitter compensation in the batching window calculation to avoid oscillation when queue depth hovers around the batching threshold.

2) Backpressure defaults (mixed profile)
- Adjust backpressure to consider both queue depth and in-flight token budget, rather than queue depth alone.
- Increase sensitivity to decode saturation so prefill does not starve indefinitely behind decode microbatches.

3) Prefill admission / fairness tweaks
- Add a lightweight fairness guard for prefill admission when decode utilization is high, prioritizing at least one prefill "slot" per scheduling cycle (when safe) to keep TTFT p95 bounded.
- Keep the existing safety checks (OOM guardrails, max in-flight tokens) and preserve current behavior for pure prefill or pure decode profiles.

4) Observability additions
- Export scheduler window and queue depth stats needed to explain benchmark deltas and to tune canary thresholds (dimensions: model, gpu_sku, kernel_mode, scheduler_profile).

Rollout / safety
- This is gated behind a server-side flag: runtime.scheduler.profile=mixed_v2 (default remains mixed_v1 until canary results are green).
- Intended rollout sequence (aligned with ENG-7431): enable in perf canary first for top-hosted models on H100/A100 in one region cohort; monitor TTFT/TPOT + error rate; then expand regions.

Benchmark results (summary)
Bench suite: top-hosted-models-benchmark-suite-v3 mixed profile (prefill/decode mix, prompt length distribution, concurrency sweep). GPU: H100 and A100. Kernel modes: baseline / FP8 / INT8 where supported.
- Primary outcome: reduced TTFT p95 variance and recovered end-to-end gains from FP8/INT8 kernels in mixed scenarios.
- Typical improvements (mixed workloads, medium concurrency): TTFT p95 -6% to -14% vs mixed_v1 with negligible TPOT impact; in several cases TPOT improved slightly due to reduced scheduler oscillation.
- Guardrail outcome: no statistically significant regressions in error rate or GPU OOM incidence across runs; GPU utilization remained within expected bounds.
Notes: raw numbers and run IDs are linked from ENG-7428 artifacts; this PR intentionally does not embed benchmark tables to avoid drift.

Key files
- serving/runtime/scheduler/defaults.cc: introduce mixed_v2 defaults and bounds
- serving/runtime/scheduler/admission_control.cc: prefill fairness guard (mixed only)
- serving/runtime/scheduler/backpressure.cc: token-budget-aware backpressure thresholds
- serving/runtime/metrics/scheduler_metrics.cc: new histograms + tags
- benchmarks/e2e/configs/mixed_workload_v3/*.yaml: updated profiles used by benchmark-lab
- docs/internal/scheduler-knobs.md: definition + rationale + override guide

Review notes / discussion highlights

text:
Title: Optimize scheduler knob defaults for mixed prefill/decode workloads

Context
During the FP8/INT8 benchmarking sprint we saw several cases where microbench wins (kernel runtime) did not translate cleanly to end-to-end improvements in the hosted runtime under mixed workloads (concurrent requests with a realistic prefill/decode mix). The main failure mode was scheduler-induced head-of-line effects: decode-heavy phases could keep the GPU busy, but TTFT (and TTFT p95 in particular) regressed because prefill admission and batching windows were tuned for "throughput-first" steady-state rather than mixed interactive traffic.

This PR updates the default scheduler knob set for the "mixed" workload profile to better realize kernel wins without paying a TTFT tail penalty. Changes were validated against the v3 top-hosted benchmark suite and compared to baseline scheduler defaults.

What changed (high level)
1) Batching window defaults (mixed profile)
- Reduce default batching_window_ms in the mixed profile (and enforce tighter min/max bounds) to reduce time spent accumulating batches when there is steady decode pressure.
- Add small jitter compensation in the batching window calculation to avoid oscillation when queue depth hovers around the batching threshold.

2) Backpressure defaults (mixed profile)
- Adjust backpressure to consider both queue depth and in-flight token budget, rather than queue depth alone.
- Increase sensitivity to decode saturation so prefill does not starve indefinitely behind decode microbatches.

3) Prefill admission / fairness tweaks
- Add a lightweight fairness guard for prefill admission when decode utilization is high, prioritizing at least one prefill "slot" per scheduling cycle (when safe) to keep TTFT p95 bounded.
- Keep the existing safety checks (OOM guardrails, max in-flight tokens) and preserve current behavior for pure prefill or pure decode profiles.

4) Observability additions
- Export scheduler window and queue depth stats needed to explain benchmark deltas and to tune canary thresholds (dimensions: model, gpu_sku, kernel_mode, scheduler_profile).

Rollout / safety
- This is gated behind a server-side flag: runtime.scheduler.profile=mixed_v2 (default remains mixed_v1 until canary results are green).
- Intended rollout sequence (aligned with ENG-7431): enable in perf canary first for top-hosted models on H100/A100 in one region cohort; monitor TTFT/TPOT + error rate; then expand regions.

Benchmark results (summary)
Bench suite: top-hosted-models-benchmark-suite-v3 mixed profile (prefill/decode mix, prompt length distribution, concurrency sweep). GPU: H100 and A100. Kernel modes: baseline / FP8 / INT8 where supported.
- Primary outcome: reduced TTFT p95 variance and recovered end-to-end gains from FP8/INT8 kernels in mixed scenarios.
- Typical improvements (mixed workloads, medium concurrency): TTFT p95 -6% to -14% vs mixed_v1 with negligible TPOT impact; in several cases TPOT improved slightly due to reduced scheduler oscillation.
- Guardrail outcome: no statistically significant regressions in error rate or GPU OOM incidence across runs; GPU utilization remained within expected bounds.
Notes: raw numbers and run IDs are linked from ENG-7428 artifacts; this PR intentionally does not embed benchmark tables to avoid drift.

Key files
- serving/runtime/scheduler/defaults.cc: introduce mixed_v2 defaults and bounds
- serving/runtime/scheduler/admission_control.cc: prefill fairness guard (mixed only)
- serving/runtime/scheduler/backpressure.cc: token-budget-aware backpressure thresholds
- serving/runtime/metrics/scheduler_metrics.cc: new histograms + tags
- benchmarks/e2e/configs/mixed_workload_v3/*.yaml: updated profiles used by benchmark-lab
- docs/internal/scheduler-knobs.md: definition + rationale + override guide

Review notes / discussion highlights
- Sofia requested we keep the fairness change narrowly scoped to mixed profile and add a kill-switch. Implemented: mixed_v2 only + flag-controlled.
- Omar asked for evidence that TPOT does not regress at higher concurrency. Added an additional concurrency sweep (64/96) in the benchmark config and updated expected ranges.
- Julian requested metric names and tags match the canary dashboards. Updated to use the standard kernel_mode/model/gpu_sku tags and added scheduler_profile.
- Rafael asked for explicit rollback triggers. Added to docs: revert to mixed_v1 via flag if TTFT p95 +8% sustained for 15m or if error rate +0.2% absolute.

Checklist
- [x] Benchmarked on H100 + A100 (mixed v3 scenarios)

- [x] Added observability hooks needed for canary
- [x] Added docs + rollout notes

title: Metrics: tag runtime perf histograms by model/GPU/kernel-mode for canary debugging
preview:
Context
We’re rolling out FP8/INT8 kernel paths behind flags and gating via perf canary. Today, the runtime exports TTFT/TPOT/tokens-per-sec histograms without enough context to quickly localize regressions. During the last canary rollback we had to correlate node labels + routing logs by hand to infer which model/GPU/mode cohort was affected.

What
This PR adds low-cardinality dimensions to a small set of runtime perf metrics so canary comparisons can be sliced directly by:
- model_id (stable registry identifier; not the human display name)
- gpu_sku (coarse SKU string, e.g., H100-SXM/A100-80GB/L4)
- kernel_mode (baseline|fp8|int8)

Key points / guardrails
- Cardinality: model_id is allowlisted to “top hosted models” (config-driven); all other models emit model_id="other". gpu_sku is derived from the node’s GPU SKU label, normalized to a small set. kernel_mode is an enum from the kernel dispatch decision (after fallback).
- Scope: only the canary-relevant histograms/counters are tagged (TTFT, TPOT, tokens/s, request_error_total). We intentionally do NOT tag every internal metric.
- Correctness: kernel_mode is recorded after dispatch/fallback so a request that fell back from FP8 due to shape guardrails is labeled kernel_mode="baseline" (this is what we want for perf attribution).
- Rollout safety: tag emission is behind a runtime config flag metrics.enable_kernel_mode_tagging (default off). We plan to enable in perf-canary first.

Implementation notes
- Added KernelMode enum to dispatch context and plumbed it through to MetricsContext.
- Added GPU SKU normalization helper (maps a handful of known strings -> canonical SKU; unknown -> "unknown").
- Added model_id extraction from ModelHandle; uses registry id (e.g., "llama-3-70b") and redacts to "other" if not allowlisted.
- Updated metrics registration to include the new label set only when the config flag is enabled; otherwise preserves current series names/labels.

Illustrative snippet
Before:
  runtime_request_ttft_ms_bucket{route="/v1/chat/completions"} …
After (when enabled):
  runtime_request_ttft_ms_bucket{route="/v1/chat/completions",model_id="llama-3-70b",gpu_sku="H100-SXM",kernel_mode="fp8"} …

Testing
- Unit tests for GPU SKU normalization and model allowlist behavior.
- Added an integration test that exercises dispatch + fallback and asserts kernel_mode label reflects the final kernel mode.
- Verified locally against a dev Prometheus scrape; series count increase stays within expected bounds for the allowlisted model set.

Follow-ups
- Grafana dashboard updates are in observability-pack PR-377.
- Canary threshold profiles tuned for the new dimensions are in perf-canary PR-611.

title: Audit log pipeline dashboards + alerts (ingestion latency, drop rate) with runbook links
preview:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization effort we’re tightening operational visibility for the audit event ingestion/export path. We’ve had a couple of near-misses where ingestion lag spiked without a clear on-call landing page, and early alert drafts lacked enough context to triage quickly.

This PR adds:
- A Grafana dashboard for the audit-log ingestion pipeline (end-to-end delivery latency, ingest backlog, drop rate, retry/429/5xx, DLQ growth).
- Alert rules for delivery latency and drop rate with sane defaults + per-environment routing.
- Runbook links embedded in alert annotations and dashboard panel descriptions.

Key signals / definitions:
- delivery_latency_seconds: time from event emission at producer to ack at ingestion sink (measured via audit-ingest gateway metrics + exporter timestamps).
- drop_rate: fraction of events not successfully delivered after retry budget (from ingest and exporter counters).
- backlog: queued events in buffer (when enabled) + ingestion consumer lag.

Alert thresholds (initial):
- AuditLogDeliveryLatencyP99High: p99(delivery_latency_seconds) > 60s for 10m (page: enterprise tiers; ticket: others).
- AuditLogDropRateHigh: drop_rate > 0.10% for 5m (page; includes per-region breakdown).
- AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing: backlog > 50k AND increasing for 15m (ticket; pages if > 200k).

Cardinality guardrails:
- Dashboard panels group primarily by region, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), and service. We intentionally do not group by raw tenant_id/project_id. event_type is only used in a single table panel with top-N + allow-listed types.

Runbooks:
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Coverage regression runbook is linked from the dashboard “Coverage” row for follow-up when events are missing but pipeline is healthy.

Testing:
- jsonnetfmt + dashboards render in CI.
- promtool rules tests added for the new alert expressions.

Notes:
- This is intended to be the “default landing page” linked from paging alerts. We can iterate thresholds after a week of production data; see INT-3984 for noise tuning follow-ups.

title: Add TTFT/TPOT threshold profiles for FP8/INT8 kernel canaries (per-SKU baselines + noise suppression)
preview: Context: As part of the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint, we’re enabling quantized kernel modes behind flags and rolling out via perf canary. The existing canary gating uses a single threshold policy that was tuned on baseline (FP16/BF16) runs and has been noisy for quantized modes (especially when sample sizes are small or when the scheduler mix shifts TTFT/TPOT differently). This PR introduces explicit threshold profiles for quantized kernels and per-SKU baselines so we can gate safely and reduce false positives while still catching real regressions. Summary of changes: (1) Add threshold profiles keyed by kernel_mode (baseline|fp8|int8) and sku (h100|a100|l4) with separate TTFT/TPOT constraints and minimum sample requirements. (2) Add noise suppression: a small-sample guardrail, robust aggregation using trimmed mean for p95 deltas, and an EWMA-based stability check that prevents flip-flopping when the signal is borderline. (3) Add a per-SKU baseline registry: baseline references are pinned by (model, sku, region, traffic_profile) so the canary compares fp8/int8 vs the correct baseline cohort rather than a global default. (4) Add explicit “capacity/variance” classification: if GPU clocks are not locked or if variance exceeds the configured bound, canary emits a WARN and does not auto-rollback (still pages on-call if error rate spikes). Default policies included (initial cut): H100 fp8 is allowed tighter TPOT deltas but slightly looser TTFT due to known launch/scheduler interactions in mixed workloads; A100 int8 is looser on TPOT until we finish shape guardrails coverage; L4 has stricter min-sample and longer evaluation window to smooth bursty workloads. Config format: new YAML profiles under config/threshold_profiles/ with schema validation in CI. Example snippet (illustrative only): profile: quantized_h100_fp8; ttft_p95_delta_pct_max: 6; tpot_p95_delta_pct_max: 3; min_requests: 2000; ewma_alpha: 0.2; trimmed_mean_pct: 10. Operational notes: This PR does NOT change any runtime flags. It only changes how perf-canary evaluates telemetry deltas when kernel_mode is fp8/int8. Rollback behavior: auto-rollback triggers remain enabled only when (a) min_requests met, (b) stability check passes (not high variance), and (c) delta breaches the configured bound for 2 consecutive windows. Otherwise we emit a blocking canary failure (no rollout progression) but do not auto-flip flags. Follow-ups: (a) once ENG-7428 matrix runs are complete we should tighten H100 fp8 TTFT; (b) add model-family overrides for known long-context TTFT sensitivity; (c) wire in the new metrics dimensions (model/gpu/kernel_mode) after runtime PR lands (tracked in ENG-7427/PR-19934 in monorepo).

title: docs(runtime): document FP8/INT8 kernel flag names + safe rollout playbook links
preview:
Context
We’ve accumulated a few FP8/INT8-related flags across the sprint (kernel integration, autoselect heuristics, and perf-canary gating). During the kickoff and variance triage we had repeated questions in Slack about:
- the canonical flag names (runtime vs runner vs perf-canary)
- which flags are safe to toggle in production vs benchmark-only
- how to stage enablement to avoid customer-impacting regressions

This PR updates internal runtime docs + flag comments so engineers and SREs can quickly find the correct knobs and the safe rollout procedure.

What’s in this PR
1) Canonical flag names + semantics (internal)
- Documented the runtime-level flags used for the FP8/INT8 sprint and clarified naming conventions.
- Added explicit “DO NOT USE in prod” notes for benchmark-only toggles.

Key flags (as documented)
- runtime.kernel_mode
  - Values: baseline | fp8 | int8 | auto
  - Notes: Prefer auto in prod; force fp8/int8 only for controlled canary/bench experiments.
- runtime.kernel_autoselect_profile
  - Values: default | fp8_int8_sprint_v1
  - Notes: fp8_int8_sprint_v1 is the new heuristics profile gated by perf canary.
- runtime.fp8.attention.enabled
  - Default: false (until canary sign-off)
  - Notes: Attention-only FP8; matmul may still be baseline/int8 depending on dispatch.
- runtime.int8.matmul.enabled
  - Default: false (until model-specific eligibility is proven)
  - Notes: Includes shape guardrails; falls back automatically when unsupported.
- runtime.quantized_kv_cache.enabled
  - Default: true
  - Notes: Alignment constraints called out; links to the relevant bugfix PR.

Perf-canary / rollout-related flags (control plane / rollout layer)
- canary.kernel_enablement.mode
  - Values: off | shadow | enabled
  - Notes: shadow compares metrics without serving customer traffic on the new path.
- canary.kernel_enablement.allowlist
  - Dimensions: model, region, sku
  - Notes: Prefer smallest viable cohort; expand by region after stability.

2) Safe rollout practices (linking to the runbook)
- Added a “Safe Rollout” section that points to the canonical Confluence runbook and summarizes the minimum steps:
  - enable behind a flag
  - start with shadow (if available) or a small cohort
  - monitor TTFT/TPOT/tokens-s, error rate, and GPU OOM/evictions
  - require an explicit rollback trigger and owner
  - expand cohort only after stable window

Confluence runbook reference
- Perf canary rollout for new kernels: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGRUNTIME/Perf+Canary+Rollout+for+New+Kernels (source: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/perf-canary-rollout-for-new-kernels.json)

3) Docs discoverability
- Updated internal docs index to include “FP8/INT8 flags” so new oncall engineers can find it from the runtime docs landing page.
- Added cross-links to:
  - kernel autoselect heuristics PR
  - perf canary gating PR
  - benchmark methodology page (for interpreting wins/regressions)

Out of scope
- No functional runtime behavior changes.
- No changes to perf-canary threshold math; only documentation of which profiles/flags exist.

Testing
- Docs build/link-check
- Grep-based CI check that referenced flags exist in the codebase (added in this PR)

Rollout / Release notes
- Internal only. No customer-facing release notes.

Related
- ENG-7431 (release plan)
- ENG-7426 (perf canary thresholds)
- PRs: #19827 (FP8 attention integration), #19841 (INT8 matmul dispatch), #19863 (autoselect heuristics), #19896 (per model/region canary gating), #19871 (KV cache alignment edge case)

title: Console: switch cost breakdown aggregation to Cost Model API (flagged + fallback + metrics)
preview: Problem: Console cost breakdown pages currently rely on legacy aggregation logic (token metering + heuristics) that can diverge from billing hooks and Optimize. This PR updates Console backend aggregation to call the internal Cost Model API v1 for per-request and aggregated breakdowns, behind a feature flag, with explicit fail-open fallback to the legacy path to protect the Console hot path. Scope: (1) New CostModelClient wiring in console-backend using the shared client library (timeouts/retries/version negotiation), (2) route-level and org-level breakdown queries now source canonical taxonomy from the cost-model service (prefill/decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, overhead allocation), (3) feature flag + gradual rollout controls, (4) metrics/tracing so we can compare legacy vs cost-model output and watch latency/error budgets. Flagging/rollout: gated by `console.cost_breakdown.use_cost_model_api` (default off), with per-tenant allowlist support for staged enablement; fallback behavior is fail-open to legacy if the cost-model call times out (>250ms), returns 4xx schema/version errors, or is in a circuit-open state due to elevated 5xx. API usage: uses `CostModelAPI.BreakdownV1` endpoint with bounded dimensions (max 20 routes, max 10 models, max 50 groups total) and requests `schema_version=1.x` (accepts unknown fields). title: Deploy gate (warn-only): surface error budget burn in CI/CD + Slack notify
preview: Context: As part of the tiered SLO + error budget enforcement rollout (Q1 2026), we need an initial “warn-only” phase that makes error budget state visible at deploy-time without blocking critical fixes. This PR adds a deploy gate step to our CI/CD pipeline that queries the current error budget state for the target tier/region, posts a warning to Slack when a tier is in a red/burning state, and logs any operator override intent (even though warn-only does not block). Scope: Hosted + Dedicated tiers; prod deploys only; Private out of scope. Behavior: (1) Determine deploy context (tier, region, env) from the deployment manifest and pipeline metadata. (2) Fetch error budget status from the SLO service (slo-control-plane) using the new `/v1/error-budget/state` endpoint (rolling 30d window, plus burn-rate annotations). (3) If state is `burning` or `exhausted`, emit a CI annotation + Slack notification with links to dashboards and the mitigation runbook. (4) If the operator provides `--override-error-budget` (or sets `ERROR_BUDGET_OVERRIDE=1`), record the override reason/link (incident URL or Linear key) to the deploy event log and emit `deploy_gate_overrides_total{tier,region,service}` for later review. (5) If the SLO service is unavailable or returns an unknown state, do NOT fail the pipeline; instead warn with a “data unavailable” message and include troubleshooting links. Implementation details: Introduces `tools/deploy-gate` (Go) used by Buildkite step `deploy_gate_error_budget_warn_only`. Adds a small shared client in `pkg/slo_client` with conservative timeouts/retries and a circuit breaker to avoid compounding an outage. Adds structured logging fields (`tier`, `region`, `service`, `budget_state`, `budget_remaining`, `burn_rate_short`, `burn_rate_long`, `override_present`, `override_reason`). Adds Slack notifier integration via existing `pkg/notify/slack` (same path used by incident-bot) and posts to `#eng-sre` and `#release-ops` for prod, with thread key `{service}:{tier}:{region}:{deploy_id}` to keep messages grouped. Links: Runbook: Confluence “Error budget breach mitigation runbook”; Policy: Confluence “Error budget policy and enforcement”; Dashboards: “Tiered SLO dashboards and annotations”. Follow-ups: This does NOT block deploys yet. Enforcement and policy tightening is tracked in ENG-4941. Notes on safety: Gate is guarded behind `deploy_gate.error_budget.enabled` (default on for prod, warn-only). Unknown/missing data never blocks in this phase. Cardinality: override metric does not include customer/model labels (only tier/region/service).

title: Post error budget burn summaries to #eng-sre on burn-rate alerts (dashboards + runbook links)
preview:
Context: As part of the tiered SLO + error budget program, we’re standardizing burn-rate alerting and enforcement. Today the burn-rate alerts page the on-call, but triage is still slow because the initial signal doesn’t include (a) how fast we’re burning, (b) what’s driving it (latency vs errors vs streaming disconnects), or (c) where to go next (dashboards/runbooks). This PR updates incident-bot to listen for burn-rate alert notifications and post a structured “burn summary” message into #eng-sre (and thread replies for updates), including deep links to the tiered SLO dashboards and the active mitigation runbook.

What this does: 1) Adds a new alert type handler for error budget burn-rate alerts (Prometheus Alertmanager payloads via our existing webhook). 2) Builds a canonical Slack message format: header with SLO name + tier + region, a short diagnosis (burn rate + window + budget remaining), and a set of links (Grafana dashboard, logs/traces, runbook). 3) Adds a lightweight “top contributors” section when the alert includes a `route` or `endpoint_class` label (to avoid cardinality explosion we only surface what’s already in the alert labels). 4) Dedupe/ratelimit: repeated firings for the same SLO/tier/region within 15 minutes update an existing thread instead of spamming the channel. 5) Safety: scrub potentially sensitive labels (customer/account identifiers) and only allow a safe-label allowlist to render in Slack.

Message contents (example): - Title: “Error budget burning: hosted / us-east-1 / chat.completions (p95 latency)” - Summary line: “Burn rate: 6.2x (2h window), budget remaining (30d): 62% → projected exhaustion in ~4.8d if sustained.” - Links: “Tiered SLO dashboard”, “Regional comparison”, “Runbook: error budget breach mitigation”, “Incident process (when to declare)”. - Footer: build/version and alert fingerprint.

Config: - Adds `BURN_SUMMARY_CHANNEL` (default #eng-sre in prod) and `BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED` feature flag (default false). - Adds `GRAFANA_BASE_URL` and `GRAFANA_DASHBOARD_UID_TIERED_SLO` for generating direct links with pre-filled variables (tier/region/endpoint_class). - Adds `RUNBOOK_URL_ERROR_BUDGET_BREACH` to keep the link stable as Confluence pages move.

Rollout plan: - Stage: enable in warn-only mode (`BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED=true`, channel #eng-sre-sandbox) for 1 week; validate formatting + dedupe behavior. - Prod: enable to #eng-sre after signoff from SRE on-call and CS escalation owners. - Follow-up: once stable, add an @-mention for the correct rotation based on tier (Dedicated vs Hosted) and severity mapping.

Out of scope: - Computing burn-rate itself (handled by recording rules + alerts in redwood/observability-pack). - Any new per-customer metrics; we explicitly avoid printing customer/account identifiers.

Refs: - Related alerting work: redwood/pr-28492-add-burn-rate-alerting-queries-and-recording-rules.json - Related dashboards: observability-pack/pr-6312-tiered-slo-dashboards-and-annotations.json - Runbook: confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/error-budget-breach-mitigation-runbook.json
