title: Add audit log lag dashboard + alerts and wire runbook links (ENG-4824)
preview:
Context: Support + CS escalations (notably around audit log delivery latency) highlighted that we lacked a first-class, Support/CS-friendly view of end-to-end audit log pipeline lag and an alert that aligns to enterprise expectations. This PR adds a dedicated dashboard and alerting rules for audit log lag, and ensures panels/alerts link to a canonical runbook. It is intended to reduce time-to-triage during tickets like SUP-1846 and to support the known-issues sprint playbook work by providing an authoritative signal.

What’s included: (1) New Grafana dashboard: "Audit Logs — Delivery Lag" with panels for ingestion → transform → delivery stages, p50/p95/p99 lag, backlog depth (where available), per-region breakdown, and a quick "is this customer-impacting" section (current lag vs expected SLO). (2) New Prometheus alerts: audit-log-lag warning + critical, scoped to production namespaces and grouped by region, with annotations that link directly to the runbook and dashboard. (3) Runbook updates: new section for audit log lag triage, plus explicit "Support-facing guidance" (what we can safely confirm, what data we should avoid sharing, and what mitigation knobs exist). (4) Routing/ownership: alerts are routed to SRE on-call; annotations include escalation guidance for Platform ownership boundaries.

Alert thresholds (aligned to enterprise expectations): - Warning: p95 delivery lag > 10m for 10m (per region) - Critical: p95 delivery lag > 30m for 10m (per region) Notes: These thresholds intentionally do not page on transient spikes; they aim to catch sustained backlog that will breach enterprise expectations. Runbook describes how to validate whether this is isolated to a subset of tenants vs global/regional.

Key design choices: - Use p95 as the primary paging signal to avoid single-tenant tail behavior dominating. Dashboard still shows p99 for investigation. - Region grouping matches how Support/CS communicate impact (us-east/eu-west/etc.) and how status events are typically framed. - Runbook contains customer-safe language; no tenant identifiers or customer names are present in dashboard queries.

Testing/validation: - Verified dashboard JSON imports cleanly into staging Grafana and renders for both "prod" and "staging" datasources. - Verified alert rule evaluation against a replayed backlog scenario (staging) triggers warning/critical as expected and resolves cleanly. - Verified links in alert annotations resolve (runbook, dashboard, and on-call channel).

Rollout: - Merge to main + apply via standard observability-pack deploy pipeline (next scheduled deploy window). - SRE on-call to monitor for noisy alerts during first 48 hours; if noisy, adjust the for-duration before adjusting thresholds.

Screenshots: Included in PR checks artifacts (Grafana render export) for quick review.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR): - Add a dedicated metric for per-tenant lag that can be surfaced only in restricted dashboards (requires security review). - Add an explicit "expected delivery SLO" panel driven by tier metadata (would require joining tier labels into metrics).

title: Prompt for + pin related SUP tickets and customer-impact summary in incident bridge channels
preview: Context: Support/SRE asked for a consistent way to keep customer escalations discoverable after a bridge ends. Today, bridge channels frequently miss links to the SUP ticket(s), and the customer-impact summary gets repeated across Slack/Jira/email with drift. This PR updates incident-bot to (1) prompt the bridge creator for related SUP tickets and a customer-impact summary, (2) post and pin a single "Escalation context" message in the bridge channel, and (3) keep the channel topic synced with SUP links (without including customer names). Behavior: - When a bridge channel is created via the bot (or when the bot detects it has been invited to a newly created bridge channel), the bot posts a message with two required actions: "Add related Jira tickets" (accepts SUP-* and optionally INT-*), and "Add customer impact" (free text w/ guidance). - Once submitted, the bot edits the message into a structured summary and pins it. - The bot also sets the channel topic to include: "SUP: SUP-xxxx, SUP-yyyy" plus a short impact tag (e.g., "impact: elevated 5xx, eu-west") and a link to the escalation playbook section on bridges. - The message includes a reminder not to paste customer-sensitive details; it focuses on symptoms/impact/request IDs. - The message provides a one-click "Update context" action to edit tickets/impact later; updates preserve history in a small "edited" footer line with timestamp + editor. Guardrails: - Only allows keys matching ^SUP-\d+$ (and ^INT-\d+$) unless the user explicitly selects "Add other Jira key" (disabled by default). - If no SUP tickets are provided within 10 minutes, the bot posts a follow-up nudge tagging the bridge creator and the current on-call group (configurable per workspace env). - If channel is moved/renamed into #incidents conventions, the pinned message remains and is re-pinned if it was unpinned. Notes: This is intentionally not doing Jira API lookups (permissions vary and we want to avoid leaking summaries into Jira unintentionally); it’s just linking + standardizing context in Slack. Follow-ups: INT-333 will enforce required fields on the Jira side; this PR is the Slack-side piece. Testing: added unit tests for parsing keys, pin idempotency, and topic formatting; added an integration test stub for Slack modal submissions (mocked Slack client).

title: Improve streaming disconnect errors (request ID propagation + clearer classification)
preview: Context: Support escalations around streaming resets/truncation (notably us-east) have been slowed by ambiguous client-visible errors and missing request IDs in some disconnect paths. This PR improves server-side error classification for streaming disconnects and ensures request ID propagation is consistent across gateway -> runtime -> client responses/logs. The goal is to let Support quickly distinguish (a) client/proxy disconnects, (b) upstream runtime cancellations, and (c) internal gateway timeouts, and to reliably hand customers a request_id they can paste back into tickets. Changes: (1) Introduce a dedicated StreamingDisconnect error category with stable public error codes and safe-to-share messaging. (2) Guarantee request_id inclusion in error payloads for streaming responses (including mid-stream termination) and in trailing metadata where applicable. (3) Improve logging: emit a single structured log line on stream termination with reason enum + timing + bytes/tokens sent (no customer payload). (4) Normalize mapping between internal cancellation reasons and public-facing error codes to reduce misclassification as 500. Notes: No customer-specific details in messages; wording reviewed with Support/CS expectations. Follow-ups: docs/SDK troubleshooting updates are tracked separately (see redwood-docs PRs).

title: Add shared Cost Model API client library (timeouts, retries, schema negotiation)
preview:
Context
Console and Optimize are both adopting the Cost Model API (see ENG-4821/4822). We need a single, opinionated client that standardizes:
- request timeouts (hot path safety for Console)
- retries (bounded + only when safe)
- schema version negotiation (so callers can be forward-compatible and we can roll schema changes safely)
- consistent auth/tenancy + tracing/metrics

What this PR does
- Adds a shared client library for service-to-service calls to Cost Model API:
  - package: //go/platform/costmodel/client (Go)
  - exported type: costmodel.Client
  - supports endpoints: EstimateCost, GetCostBreakdown, WhatIf
- Timeouts
  - default request timeout: 250ms (overridable per-call)
  - default connect timeout: 50ms
  - caller can pass a context with deadline; client will respect the stricter of (ctx deadline, configured timeout)
- Retries
  - retries are bounded: max 2 retries, exponential backoff + full jitter
  - only retries on: 502/503/504, connection reset/timeouts, and explicit server retryable errors
  - never retries on: 4xx (except 429), schema/version errors, validation errors
  - idempotency: for POST requests we attach X-Redwood-Request-Id; server can safely dedupe if it chooses (client side still assumes retries may result in duplicate work)
  - 429 behavior: honor Retry-After (bounded by a per-call max budget)
- Schema version negotiation
  - client sends:
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version: <preferred_version>
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Versions-Accepted: <csv of supported versions>
  - server responds with:
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version-Applied: <applied_version>
  - client surfaces applied version in response metadata and emits a metric when fallback occurs (preferred != applied)
  - unknown response fields are ignored by default (forward compatibility); strict mode is available for conformance tests
- Auth/tenancy plumbing
  - client requires tenant context (tenant_id) and propagates:
    - X-Redwood-Tenant-Id
    - X-Redwood-Caller-Service (for attribution)
  - supports standard internal service auth transport (mTLS) and attaches per-service auth token when available via ctx
- Observability
  - OpenTelemetry spans: costmodel.client.<method>
  - metrics:
    - costmodel_client_requests_total{method,status,retried}
    - costmodel_client_latency_ms_bucket{method}
    - costmodel_client_schema_applied_total{preferred,applied}
    - costmodel_client_retry_total{method,reason}
  - adds log fields (structured): request_id, tenant_id, schema_preferred, schema_applied

text:
Context
Console and Optimize are both adopting the Cost Model API (see ENG-4821/4822). We need a single, opinionated client that standardizes:
- request timeouts (hot path safety for Console)
- retries (bounded + only when safe)
- schema version negotiation (so callers can be forward-compatible and we can roll schema changes safely)
- consistent auth/tenancy + tracing/metrics

What this PR does
- Adds a shared client library for service-to-service calls to Cost Model API:
  - package: //go/platform/costmodel/client (Go)
  - exported type: costmodel.Client
  - supports endpoints: EstimateCost, GetCostBreakdown, WhatIf
- Timeouts
  - default request timeout: 250ms (overridable per-call)
  - default connect timeout: 50ms
  - caller can pass a context with deadline; client will respect the stricter of (ctx deadline, configured timeout)
- Retries
  - retries are bounded: max 2 retries, exponential backoff + full jitter
  - only retries on: 502/503/504, connection reset/timeouts, and explicit server retryable errors
  - never retries on: 4xx (except 429), schema/version errors, validation errors
  - idempotency: for POST requests we attach X-Redwood-Request-Id; server can safely dedupe if it chooses (client side still assumes retries may result in duplicate work)
  - 429 behavior: honor Retry-After (bounded by a per-call max budget)
- Schema version negotiation
  - client sends:
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version: <preferred_version>
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Versions-Accepted: <csv of supported versions>
  - server responds with:
    - X-Redwood-Cost-Model-Schema-Version-Applied: <applied_version>
  - client surfaces applied version in response metadata and emits a metric when fallback occurs (preferred != applied)
  - unknown response fields are ignored by default (forward compatibility); strict mode is available for conformance tests
- Auth/tenancy plumbing
  - client requires tenant context (tenant_id) and propagates:
    - X-Redwood-Tenant-Id
    - X-Redwood-Caller-Service (for attribution)
  - supports standard internal service auth transport (mTLS) and attaches per-service auth token when available via ctx
- Observability
  - OpenTelemetry spans: costmodel.client.<method>
  - metrics:
    - costmodel_client_requests_total{method,status,retried}
    - costmodel_client_latency_ms_bucket{method}
    - costmodel_client_schema_applied_total{preferred,applied}
    - costmodel_client_retry_total{method,reason}
  - adds log fields (structured): request_id, tenant_id, schema_preferred, schema_applied

Why now
We have multiple in-flight integrations (Console + Optimize) and we’ve already seen early endpoint performance discussions in eng-platform threads. Centralizing client behavior prevents each consumer from inventing its own timeouts/retry logic and makes schema rollouts measurable.

Not in scope
- circuit breaking (we’ll rely on existing service mesh / caller-side fail-open behavior per integration)
- client-side caching (Console caching guidance will live in the integration how-to)
- non-Go clients (we can port the semantics to TS later; this PR establishes the canonical behavior)

Test plan
- Unit tests for:
  - retry policy (retryable vs non-retryable statuses)
  - 429 Retry-After handling
  - schema negotiation header behavior + strict mode
  - context deadline behavior
- Integration-style tests with httptest server verifying:
  - headers for tenant/auth/version negotiation
  - metrics/spans are emitted

Rollout
- Library is additive.
- Console/Optimize adoption will be done in separate PRs behind feature flags.

Follow-ups

- Evaluate lowering default timeout for Console once we have p95 data in the new dashboards

title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
preview: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene

title: Add incident taxonomy dashboards + alerting (type/owner breakdowns + playbook links)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds the initial Incident Taxonomy (v1) dashboards + a small set of alert rules, per the reliability program initiative. The goal is to make taxonomy classification measurable and to enable reporting/triage by incident_type + owner_team without exploding metric label cardinality.

Key deliverables in this PR:
- Grafana dashboard: **Incident Taxonomy (v1)**
  - incident volume and impact proxy panels (by tier, severity)
  - breakdowns by **incident_type** and **owner_team**
  - classification compliance (% unknown/unclassified)
  - MTTA/MTTR approximations using incident event timestamps
  - drilldowns from type -> owner -> recent incidents (table)
  - playbook links based on incident_type (panel links + table column)
- Prometheus rules:
  - recording rules to normalize/aggregate incident event counters into a stable set of labels
  - alert rules for (1) unclassified rate regression (2) spike of incidents by type/owner (3) sustained active incidents for Dedicated/Private

## Context / Motivation
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), incident_type/subtype and owner are now required fields in incident tooling. We need dashboards/alerts that:
- show whether classification is being used correctly (and where it’s not)
- create a feedback loop for top recurring incident categories
- allow on-call to quickly jump from “what’s happening” -> “who owns it” -> “what runbook should I follow”

This PR targets the spec in Confluence (Incident taxonomy dashboards spec) and ties into Linear ENG-4828.

## Design notes (cardinality + data sources)
Data sources used:
- `incident_events_total{event=...}` emitted from incident-bot pipeline / annotations ingestion
- existing `request_errors_total` and `request_latency_seconds` are NOT relabeled here; the dashboard focuses on incidents as the unit of analysis

Cardinality constraints:
- `incident_type` is treated as a strict enum (v1 allowlist)
- `owner_team` is constrained to a small allowlist of on-call teams (e.g., SRE, Fleet, Routing, Runtime, Observability, SecurityOps, PrivateDeploy)
- `incident_subtype` is intentionally not used as a Prometheus label in this PR (shown only in the incident table sourced from annotations/events)
- `unknown` is a first-class bucket to avoid missing data (explicitly tracked)

## What changed
### Dashboards
- New dashboard: `dashboards/incident-taxonomy-v1.jsonnet`
  - Variables: `tier`, `severity`, `owner_team`, `incident_type`, `env`, `region`
  - Panels:
    - “Incidents created (last 24h/7d) by incident_type”
    - “Incidents created by owner_team”
    - “Active incidents over time” (stacked by tier)
    - “Classification compliance” (% known vs unknown)
    - “Top incident types (28d) + median time-to-mitigate”
    - “Recent incidents” table (links to Slack channel + incident doc + runbook)
  - Playbook links:
    - Uses a small mapping file to link type -> runbook URL (SRE/Infra/Runtime runbooks)

### Prometheus rules
- `prometheus/rules/incident_taxonomy_recording_rules.yaml`
  - Normalizes `incident_events_total` into:
    - `ri:incidents_created:count{tier,severity,incident_type,owner_team}`
    - `ri:incidents_active:count{tier,severity,incident_type,owner_team}`
    - `ri:incidents_unclassified:count{tier,severity}`
- `prometheus/alerts/incident_taxonomy_alerts.yaml`
  - `IncidentTaxonomyUnclassifiedRateHigh` (warn): unclassified >= 10% over 6h (all tiers)
  - `IncidentSpikeByTypeOwner` (warn): 3x baseline over 2h for any (incident_type, owner_team) in Hosted
  - `SustainedActiveIncidentsDedicatedPrivate` (page): active incidents >= 2 for 30m for Dedicated/Private

## Example queries (for reviewers)
- Incidents created by type:
  - `sum by (incident_type) (increase(ri:incidents_created:count{tier="$tier"}[24h]))`
- Unclassified rate:
  - `sum(increase(ri:incidents_unclassified:count{tier="$tier"}[6h])) / sum(increase(ri:incidents_created:count{tier="$tier"}[6h]))`

## Screenshots
(Attached in PR UI)
- Incident Taxonomy (v1) overview
- Drilldown: Routing-owned incidents, last 14d
- Classification compliance panel with unknown bucket

## Rollout / Ops
- Safe to deploy behind existing dashboard folder permissions.
- Alert routing:
  - warn alerts to `#eng-sre` (triage)
  - page alerts to `#incidents` + paging policy for SRE primary (owner_team is informational; paging by owner will be part of ENG-4823)

## Testing
- `make lint`
- `make dashboards-test` (jsonnet render)
- `promtool test rules`
- Verified panels render in staging Grafana (sre-observability)

## Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Reduce/lock down any remaining label surfaces if we see unexpected churn in `owner_team` values (see review notes).
- Add saved views per tier/team once CS permissions model is finalized.
- Add explicit MTTR SLA breach overlays once remediation SLA timers land (ENG-4829).

---
Closes: ENG-4828

title: perf-canary: add warning-level alert rules (dampening + min sample size) routed to #eng-runtime
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial set of warning-level perf-canary alerts (no paging) to detect per-model serving performance regressions early, with explicit guardrails to reduce noise during ramp.

This PR wires:
- Warning alerts for p95 latency regression and tokens/sec drops
- Dampening (sustained windows) + min-sample conditions
- Alertmanager routing to Slack #eng-runtime
- Runbook + dashboard links in annotations

This is intended to be enabled first in us-east with a limited model list (per ENG-4880 rollout plan). Paging rules are tracked separately (pr-19183).

## Motivation / context
During early perf-canary rollout we saw false positives driven by low volume, baseline drift, and transient telemetry delays. The warning rules in this PR aim to be:
- Low-noise: only fire when there’s enough data
- Actionable: include baseline vs observed deltas + direct links
- Safe: warning-only channel routing while we calibrate

## Alerting approach (v0)
### What we alert on
1) **p95 end-to-end latency regression** (primary): observed p95 vs baseline p95
2) **tokens/sec drop** (secondary): observed tokens/sec vs baseline tokens/sec

### Key guardrails
- **Minimum sample size**: require >= N completed canary requests in the evaluation window
- **Dampening**: require condition to hold for a continuous duration before firing
- **Cardinality limits**: alert labels only include model + region + tier + runtime_build_id (no prompt-shape label)

### Windows / thresholds (v0 defaults)
- p95 latency regression: >= +12% for 15m (warning)
- tokens/sec drop: <= -10% for 15m (warning)
- min samples: >= 200 requests in 15m per (model, region, tier)

These defaults are intentionally conservative; per-model overrides will be managed via the baseline/threshold config in the perf-canary service and/or follow-up PR if we need rule-level overrides.

## Implementation details
- Adds alerting rules under `alerts/perf_canary/warnings.yaml`
- Adds an Alertmanager route/receiver for `slack_eng_runtime` with a grouping key on (alertname, model, region, tier)
- Adds templates used to render compact Slack messages including baseline/observed/threshold and deep links

### Example rule snippet
The core pattern is:

- Ensure sufficient volume:
  `sum(increase(perf_canary_requests_total{status="ok"}[15m])) by (model, region, tier) >= 200`

- Compare observed p95 to baseline p95 (both expressed in ms):
  `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(perf_canary_e2e_latency_ms_bucket[5m])) by (le, model, region, tier)) / on(model, region, tier) group_left perf_canary_baseline_p95_latency_ms`

- Fire when ratio exceeds threshold for long enough:
  `> 1.12 for: 15m`

Annotations include:
- `runbook_url`: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Runbook%3A+Perf+Regression+Canary+Alert
- `dashboard_url`: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/perf-canary/perf-canary?var-model={{ $labels.model }}&var-region={{ $labels.region }}

## Testing
- `promtool check rules` in CI
- `promtool test rules` added/updated for:
  - happy path: regression sustained with min samples -> fires
  - low-volume path: regression but samples < min -> does not fire
  - transient spike: regression for < for-window -> does not fire
- Alertmanager config validation

## Rollout plan
1) Merge rules disabled by default (gated by `perf_canary_alerts_enabled` config in the alerting pipeline)
2) Enable in us-east for 2–3 models with only warning routing
3) Tune thresholds/min-samples if needed (ENG-4882)
4) Add paging rules separately once warning noise is acceptable (pr-19183)

## Risks / mitigations
- Risk: telemetry ingestion delay causes late datapoints and apparent regressions
  - Mitigation: long `for:` window + min samples; dashboard links include ingestion-lag panel (from pr-19174)
- Risk: baseline drift causes slow burn alerts
  - Mitigation: warning-only; follow-up ADR work for baseline update policy

## Screenshots
N/A (rule-based); see CI output for promtool rule tests.

## Checklist
- [x] Warning alerts only (no paging)
- [x] Min sample size guardrails
- [x] Dampening windows
- [x] Slack routing to #eng-runtime
- [x] Runbook + dashboard links
- [x] promtool validation + tests

Related:
- Dashboards: pr-19174
- Paging rules: pr-19183
- Perf-canary payload formatting: perf-canary pr-19244

title: Runtime: KV compaction kill-switch + config plumbing (oncall-safe disable path)
preview:
Context: KV cache compaction/eviction is rolling out behind flags. During canaries we need a deterministic, oncall-friendly way to disable compaction immediately (or gate it to a safe minimum) without requiring a full runtime restart, while preserving allocator invariants and avoiding relocation-in-progress hazards.

This PR adds the config/feature-flag plumbing and a safe-disable path (“kill switch”) for KV compaction + related relocation behavior. It also wires metrics/tracing so oncall can validate that the disable took effect, and links to the runtime runbook + rollout guardrails.

What changed: (1) Introduced runtime config keys under `kv.compaction.*` with sane defaults for release/runtime-1.22: `kv.compaction.enabled` (bool), `kv.compaction.kill_switch` (bool, higher priority than enabled), `kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick` (int), `kv.compaction.budget_bytes_per_tick` (int), `kv.compaction.min_fragmentation_ratio` (float), and `kv.compaction.force_background_worker_off` (bool). (2) Added control-plane config subscription wiring to the runtime config registry so these keys can be flipped via existing rollout tooling; includes “last applied config version” and “last applied timestamp” tracking. (3) Implemented safe-disable semantics: kill-switch causes (a) scheduler to stop granting compaction budget immediately, (b) background worker to stop accepting new relocation tasks, and (c) relocation state machine to transition to a quiescent point (finish current page move if already in a copy/commit section, then stop). The goal is to avoid leaving partially-relocated blocks or dangling references; correctness is preferred over instantly aborting mid-copy. (4) Added oncall-exposed counters + gauges: `kv_compaction_config_kill_switch{}` (0/1), `kv_compaction_config_enabled{}` (0/1), `kv_compaction_config_version` (gauge), `kv_compaction_disable_inflight_moves_total` (counter), and `kv_compaction_disable_wait_ms` (histogram) so we can see whether we were blocked waiting to reach a safe point. (5) Tracing: added a lightweight span event `kv_compaction.kill_switch_applied` with config version + node id to correlate with any tail-latency spikes around a disable. (6) Docs pointers: updated internal docs links in code comments to `sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook.json` and `hosted-api-tail-latency-guardrails.json` (SRE guardrails). No user-facing docs.

Operational behavior (intended): - If `kill_switch=true`, compaction does not run regardless of `enabled`. - If `enabled=false` (kill_switch false), compaction is disabled but we still export config metrics; relocation state machine remains loaded but idle. - If config propagation is delayed, nodes continue with previous config; metrics include applied version so rollout system can detect stragglers. - On shutdown/restart, persisted config is re-read at boot, so kill-switch is sticky across restarts.

Notes on safety: Kill-switch does not hard-cancel a relocation that is already in a critical section. Instead we wait for a “commit boundary” (copy -> pointer swap -> free old) and then stop. This avoids a correctness class where we’d lose KV pages or leave allocator free-list inconsistent. Oncall expectation: disable should apply within <100ms under normal load; we record if it takes longer.

Files/areas touched (high level): - `runtime/config/kv_compaction_config.{h,cc}` new config keys + validation - `runtime/control_plane/config_subscriber.cc` registry wiring + version tracking - `runtime/kv/compaction/compaction_worker.cc` kill-switch checks + quiesce - `runtime/kv/compaction/relocation_state_machine.cc` quiescent boundary handling - `runtime/scheduler/scheduler_loop.cc` compaction budget gating on kill-switch - `runtime/observability/metrics_kv_compaction.cc` config + disable metrics - `runtime/tracing/kv_compaction_spans.cc` event emission - tests: `runtime/kv/compaction/test_kill_switch.cc`, `runtime/config/test_kv_config_validation.cc`

Small illustrative snippet: `if (cfg.kv.compaction.kill_switch) { budget = 0; worker->RequestQuiesce("kill_switch"); }`

Testing: - Unit: config validation, kill-switch override ordering, quiesce boundary behavior - Integration: scheduler loop doesn’t grant budget with kill-switch on, even with fragmentation trigger - Soak: repeated flip enabled/disabled/kill_switch under synthetic churn; asserts no allocator invariant violations - Manual: verified config flip via rollout tool updates metrics within propagation window

Rollout plan: This is a prerequisite for canarying compaction allocator changes (PR-19357/19371). Landed in release/runtime-1.22 so we can use it during canary expansions. Kill-switch authority remains SRE oncall (per guardrails doc), with runtime TL as escalation.

Links: - Linear: ENG-4826 (kill-switch/config), ENG-4829 (release plan) - Jira: INT-11904 (dashboard standards follow-up), SUP-28433 (OOM/high churn mitigation path) - Related PRs: pr-19357 (allocator changes), pr-19371 (scheduler budgeting), pr-19405 (tracing spans)

Review conversation (highlights): - Sean Gallagher: asked for explicit metrics proving kill-switch applied + a bound on “time to quiesce.” Added `disable_wait_ms` histogram and documented expected behavior in comments. - Rafael Mendes: requested oncall steps + clarity on whether kill-switch persists across restart. Updated config boot path and added a note in runtime runbook link comment; verified sticky behavior. - Omar El-Sayed: questioned whether we should abort mid-move to reduce tail latency during incident. We chose correctness-first and added the counter `disable_inflight_moves_total` so we can see when we had to wait. - Allison Grant: asked to align metric naming with standards; renamed gauges to `kv_compaction_config_*` and added labels consistent with other runtime configs.

CI: First run failed `cfg-lint` due to missing docs link in new config file; fixed and reran. Final checks green: build (linux), unit tests, integration tests, sanitizer, config-lint.

Merge: Squash-merged into release/runtime-1.22. Backport not required (feature is new).

title: Console backend: add internal admin hooks to view tier limits + recent overload events
preview:
Context: As part of hot-route capacity protection rollout, on-call/SRE and platform operators need a quick way (from Console) to answer two recurring questions during incidents and enterprise escalations: (1) what tier limits/budgets are currently configured for a given route group and region, and (2) have we recently activated overload protection (admission control / shedding) and why. This PR adds backend-only hooks for an internal operator view (not exposed in public UI yet) and keeps the data/authorization boundaries tight.

What’s in this PR: Adds internal admin GraphQL resolvers and service-layer functions to (a) fetch the effective tier limit configuration for a route group (including defaults + per-route overrides after config migration) and (b) retrieve a bounded, low-cardinality list of recent overload events for that route group, with tier attribution and reason codes aligned with the new 429 semantics. These endpoints are intended for internal operators only; UI work is explicitly out-of-scope.

Key changes: 1) GraphQL (console-backend): - New queries: adminTierLimits(routeGroupId, region) and adminOverloadEvents(routeGroupId, region, lookbackMinutes=60, limit=200). - Response payloads include: tier class (enterprise/best-effort/internal), rpm burst window metadata, quota window metadata (when present), and a computed “effectiveLimitSource” (default vs override) to help debug drift. - Overload events include: timestamp, signal type (queue_depth/scheduler_lag/gpu_saturation), action (admission_control/shed), tier, reason_code (matches gateway 429 reason codes), and sample counts aggregated per minute.

2) AuthZ and safety: - All admin queries are gated behind existing Console RBAC: requires role CONSOLE_INTERNAL_OPERATOR or SRE_ONCALL, and is additionally guarded by feature flag console.adminOverloadDebug (default off in prod; enabled via allowlist during rollout windows). - Added audit log entries for each admin query invocation (actor, org, routeGroupId, region, lookbackMinutes) per INT-3302 requirements.

3) Data sources: - Tier limit config is read from the policy/config service via the existing typed client; we request the “effective config” view to avoid re-implementing merge logic in Console. - Overload events are queried from telemetry aggregates (overload_events_v2 rollup) rather than raw event streams to avoid cardinality/latency risks; lookback and limit are hard-bounded server-side.

4) Failure behavior: - If telemetry is unavailable or the aggregate query times out, adminOverloadEvents returns a partial response with a warning field and empty events list (and emits a console-backend metric admin_overload_events_fetch_error_total with error_class tag). - If the policy service is unreachable, adminTierLimits fails closed with a 503 and a stable error code CONSOLE_ADMIN_POLICY_UNAVAILABLE (so on-call can distinguish from auth failures).

Why this approach: We intentionally keep enforcement and decisioning out of Console. Console is read-only here: it reflects the effective configuration and recent system behavior with minimal overhead. Pulling aggregates (not raw events) keeps the request path predictable and reduces the chance that the operator tool becomes part of an incident blast radius.

Out of scope: - Public UI: no customer-facing pages, no SDK/API exposure. - Writing/updating tier limits from Console (future work, requires a stricter change-management flow). - Linking directly to the admission-decision debug endpoint (handled in a separate PR with tighter access controls).

Testing: - Unit tests for resolvers: auth gating, flag gating, bounds enforcement, and error mapping. - Integration tests (mocked policy + mocked telemetry client) verifying effective-config passthrough and rollup query shape. - Manual: verified locally against staging policy service and staging telemetry aggregates for us-east canary accounts.

Observability: - Added latency metrics for the two admin queries and a rate metric to watch for accidental high-volume usage. - Audit logs are emitted to the existing internal-audit sink with event names console.admin.tierLimits.read and console.admin.overloadEvents.read.

Docs/notes: Added an internal README snippet in console-backend describing how to enable the flag during an incident and how to interpret fields. This will be folded into the SRE runbook after UI is live.

Related: ENG-4926 (dashboards/alerts), ENG-4928 (feature-flagging/rollout guardrails), INT-3302 (admin access + audit requirements).

title: Add hot-route protection dashboards + alert rules (enterprise tier + shed reasons)
preview: Context: As part of Hot-route Capacity Protection (ENG-4926), SRE needs a consistent set of dashboards + alert rules for protected routes (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank) that make it easy to answer: (1) are enterprise routes staying within SLO under fleet pressure, (2) are we shedding/throttling the right tier first, and (3) what is the reason breakdown (quota vs overload vs admission control) when 429s occur. This PR adds Grafana dashboard definitions (jsonnet) and Prometheus alert rules for the new metrics emitted by gateway/routing/runtime (see redwood PRs for instrumentation and 429 reason codes). Scope: dashboards (per-route/per-tier latency + error + shed), annotations for protection activation events, and paging rules focused on enterprise degradation (not expected best-effort shedding). What’s included: (A) Dashboards: 1) Hot Route Protection – Overview: request volume by route group + tier, 429 rate by reason, shed/admission-control rate, queue depth / scheduler lag signals, and 5xx. 2) Hot Route Protection – Enterprise SLO: p50/p95/p99 latency by protected route group for enterprise tier, availability (2xx/3xx) and error budget burn panels, with burn windows (5m/1h/6h). 3) Hot Route Protection – Debug: top tenants (sampled/low-cardinality) by route group, saturation indicators, and correlation panels for policy-eval latency + routing decision outcomes. (B) Alerts (Prometheus rules): 1) Page: enterprise protected route latency burn > 2x for 15m OR fast burn > 10x for 5m (gated to enterprise tier + protected route groups). 2) Page: enterprise protected route 5xx rate > 1% for 10m (excluding client cancellations). 3) Ticket: sustained overload protection activation (shed_rate_overload_reason > threshold for 30m) when enterprise SLO remains healthy (expected during pressure) to drive capacity follow-up without paging storms. 4) Ticket: missing/flatlined metrics for new overload reason series (helps catch ingestion regressions and the cardinality hotfix class of issues). Notes/guardrails: All queries follow the overload metrics cardinality guidance: no tenant_id in primary panels; we use route_group, tier, region, reason_code only. The “Debug” dashboard includes an opt-in variable for sampled tenant (uses pre-aggregated topK series). Alert rules intentionally avoid paging on best-effort 429 spikes alone; we page only when enterprise SLO signals degrade. Rollout: dashboards are safe to merge before full multi-region enablement; panels will show ‘No data’ until metrics are present. Alert rules are shipped disabled by default via env toggle in the pack; enablement to follow GA checklist (eng-releases thread + canary results). Links: Linear ENG-4926 (dashboards/alerts), ENG-4927 (incident-bot notifications). Related: slo-toolkit PR for standard burn alert helper functions, redwood PR adding overload reason codes to 429s, and runtime PR exporting queue depth + scheduler lag.

title: Rollout eu-west protected routes and tune overload thresholds
preview:
Context: We enabled hot-route capacity protection in us-east canary and validated that enterprise budgets remain stable under best-effort pressure. This PR extends the same protection to eu-west and tunes overload thresholds based on eu-west observed traffic (higher long-prompt share on /v1/chat/completions and more bursty embeddings spikes in the morning UTC window). The primary goal is to preserve enterprise SLO behavior without inducing unnecessary best-effort shedding or paging noise.

What this PR does: (1) Enables the eu-west protected route group behind the region-scoped flag hot_route_protection.enabled and wires the eu-west fleet signal feed into the routing admission controller. (2) Tunes eu-west admission thresholds (queue depth + scheduler lag) using percentile distributions captured over the last 7 days; in eu-west the same absolute queue-depth thresholds caused premature best-effort throttling during benign bursts due to different batching dynamics. (3) Adds guardrails: enterprise allowlist expansion requires a config gate + SRE approval annotation and adds an explicit rollback trigger when sustained enterprise p95 > SLO target for 10m while protection is active. (4) Updates SRE dashboard annotations to mark protection activation and the active threshold profile for eu-west.

Tuning details (eu-west profile): - Admission controller trigger (best-effort gating): queue_depth_p95 >= 120 for 2m OR scheduler_lag_ms_p95 >= 85 for 2m (was 90 / 70 in us-east). - Shedding trigger (best-effort controlled shedding): queue_depth_p99 >= 220 for 1m OR scheduler_lag_ms_p99 >= 160 for 1m (was 190 / 140 in us-east). - Enterprise budget reservation remains unchanged; only best-effort policy thresholds are adjusted. - Minimum hold-down: 60s once gating begins to avoid oscillation during spiky embeddings traffic.

Rollout plan: - Stage 0: Deploy config + code in eu-west with hot_route_protection.enabled=false (default). - Stage 1: Enable flag in eu-west for internal/test tenants only; verify dashboards and incident-bot messages. - Stage 2: Enable for allowlisted enterprise accounts on protected routes (initial: 3 tenants coordinated via TAM/CS). - Stage 3: Expand allowlist to remaining eu-west enterprise tenants once burn-rate alerts remain green for 48h.

Risk/mitigations: - Risk: threshold mismatch causing unnecessary 429s for best-effort. Mitigation: tuned thresholds + hold-down; dashboards show shed reasons and admission decisions. - Risk: missed enterprise degradation due to gating masking underlying fleet issues. Mitigation: explicit rollback trigger on enterprise p95 + error-budget burn; paging remains tied to enterprise SLOs rather than shed rate. - Risk: metric cardinality regression from region labels. Mitigation: tags remain bounded (region, route_group, tier, reason); no tenant tags added.

Testing: - Unit: routing policy evaluation tests updated for eu-west profile selection. - Integration: ran policy simulation for eu-west profile against captured request-shape distributions (see linked Linear notes). - Load: replayed embeddings spike scenario; verified enterprise traffic stays within budget while best-effort gets 429 overload_shed with Retry-After.

text:
Context: We enabled hot-route capacity protection in us-east canary and validated that enterprise budgets remain stable under best-effort pressure. This PR extends the same protection to eu-west and tunes overload thresholds based on eu-west observed traffic (higher long-prompt share on /v1/chat/completions and more bursty embeddings spikes in the morning UTC window). The primary goal is to preserve enterprise SLO behavior without inducing unnecessary best-effort shedding or paging noise.

What this PR does: (1) Enables the eu-west protected route group behind the region-scoped flag hot_route_protection.enabled and wires the eu-west fleet signal feed into the routing admission controller. (2) Tunes eu-west admission thresholds (queue depth + scheduler lag) using percentile distributions captured over the last 7 days; in eu-west the same absolute queue-depth thresholds caused premature best-effort throttling during benign bursts due to different batching dynamics. (3) Adds guardrails: enterprise allowlist expansion requires a config gate + SRE approval annotation and adds an explicit rollback trigger when sustained enterprise p95 > SLO target for 10m while protection is active. (4) Updates SRE dashboard annotations to mark protection activation and the active threshold profile for eu-west.

Tuning details (eu-west profile): - Admission controller trigger (best-effort gating): queue_depth_p95 >= 120 for 2m OR scheduler_lag_ms_p95 >= 85 for 2m (was 90 / 70 in us-east). - Shedding trigger (best-effort controlled shedding): queue_depth_p99 >= 220 for 1m OR scheduler_lag_ms_p99 >= 160 for 1m (was 190 / 140 in us-east). - Enterprise budget reservation remains unchanged; only best-effort policy thresholds are adjusted. - Minimum hold-down: 60s once gating begins to avoid oscillation during spiky embeddings traffic.

Rollout plan: - Stage 0: Deploy config + code in eu-west with hot_route_protection.enabled=false (default). - Stage 1: Enable flag in eu-west for internal/test tenants only; verify dashboards and incident-bot messages. - Stage 2: Enable for allowlisted enterprise accounts on protected routes (initial: 3 tenants coordinated via TAM/CS). - Stage 3: Expand allowlist to remaining eu-west enterprise tenants once burn-rate alerts remain green for 48h.

Risk/mitigations: - Risk: threshold mismatch causing unnecessary 429s for best-effort. Mitigation: tuned thresholds + hold-down; dashboards show shed reasons and admission decisions. - Risk: missed enterprise degradation due to gating masking underlying fleet issues. Mitigation: explicit rollback trigger on enterprise p95 + error-budget burn; paging remains tied to enterprise SLOs rather than shed rate. - Risk: metric cardinality regression from region labels. Mitigation: tags remain bounded (region, route_group, tier, reason); no tenant tags added.

Testing: - Unit: routing policy evaluation tests updated for eu-west profile selection. - Integration: ran policy simulation for eu-west profile against captured request-shape distributions (see linked Linear notes). - Load: replayed embeddings spike scenario; verified enterprise traffic stays within budget while best-effort gets 429 overload_shed with Retry-After.

Notes: This is config-heavy; reviewers please focus on threshold values, hold-down behavior, and rollback gates. Code changes are minimal and mostly wiring/profile selection.

title: Post-launch cleanup: remove legacy route throttle path
preview:
Context: After GA enablement of tier-aware rate limiting/quota enforcement + priority routing for protected routes, we now have two overlapping throttling code paths in the API gateway: (1) the new PolicyEngine-backed tier-aware enforcement (route groups + priority classes + standardized 429 semantics) and (2) a legacy per-route throttle path that predates hot-route protection. Keeping both paths has caused operational confusion (which one triggered?) and maintenance risk (duplicate config, inconsistent headers, and drift in retry semantics). This PR removes the legacy throttle path and consolidates all enforcement to the new tier-aware enforcement, while keeping compatibility shims for the few remaining callers/config entries.

What changed: Remove legacy throttle middleware and its config plumbing; delete deprecated route-level throttle config keys and migration fallback logic; consolidate all request admission decisions behind the PolicyEngine-backed evaluator (quota + rate + overload admission control) for /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, and /v1/rerank route groups; ensure a single 429 response schema (reason codes + headers) is used regardless of whether the decision was quota, rate, or overload shedding; unify metric emission so `overload.*` and `gateway.admission.*` counters are emitted exactly once per request with consistent tier attribution; update the internal admin debug endpoint to report only the new decision sources (removes legacy flags from output) to reduce ambiguity during on-call.

Why now: We saw multiple support/escalation threads where customers (and on-call) could not distinguish legacy throttles from hot-route protection 429s. SUP-18421 is a concrete example where investigation time was longer due to mixed headers/metrics across the two paths.

Risk & mitigation: The main risk is behavior change for routes that still had legacy throttles configured (especially some internal/proxy routes). Mitigations: (a) config migration keeps the old keys as no-ops with warnings for one release cycle; (b) a canary flag `gateway.enforcement.only_policy_engine` is introduced and is default OFF in this PR, then enabled in staging and canary; (c) integration tests cover legacy-config-present cases to ensure they map to the tier-aware evaluator or are rejected at config-load time with actionable errors; (d) metrics and headers were compared in staging load tests to ensure parity for quota/rate 429s.

Testing: Unit tests for config parsing/migration and 429 schema; integration tests for chat/embeddings/rerank under concurrent load (quota window + rate limit + overload admission control); staging load test replay of representative burst traffic (mixed enterprise + best-effort) verifying enterprise SLO stability and expected best-effort shedding; manual validation via admin debug endpoint.

Rollout plan: Merge behind flag; enable in staging for 24h; enable in us-east canary allowlist (existing protected-route canary accounts) during a staffed window; then roll out region-by-region (us-east -> eu-west -> us-west) with dashboards/alerts monitored. Rollback is flipping `gateway.enforcement.only_policy_engine` OFF (restores previous behavior without re-deploy).

Follow-ups: (1) After one release cycle, remove the deprecated config key warning shim and fail-fast on legacy keys; (2) update the internal runbook section on "throttle source" to remove references to the legacy path.

Checklist: Added/updated tests; updated metrics to avoid duplicate emission; validated 429 headers and reason codes; confirmed no new high-cardinality tags; docs not required (internal behavior).

title: Add synthetic canary for graceful degradation path (model/region fallback)
preview: Summary: This adds a new synthetic canary test that exercises Redwood’s graceful degradation behavior (fallback model and/or fallback region) and emits low-cardinality signals that map directly to incident taxonomy categories (Routing/Fallback misbehavior, Capacity/Fleet shortage, Control plane/routing config). Motivation: Recent incidents and taxonomy workshops highlighted that we don’t currently have a reliable, continuous signal that the “degrade gracefully” path is actually working end-to-end (router decision -> fallback selection -> request succeeds -> correct fallback_reason emitted). This canary is intentionally not a throughput/perf benchmark; it is a correctness + reliability smoke test with a small number of requests on a fixed cadence. What it does: (1) Sends a small chat completion request using a policy that prefers a primary model/region, but is allowed to fall back to a designated compatible model and a designated secondary region. (2) Validates that the response is successful and that the response metadata includes a structured fallback_reason when a fallback occurs (or explicitly indicates no fallback when not needed). (3) Publishes Prometheus metrics and structured logs that can be used in incident tooling/dashboards without exploding label cardinality. Key signals emitted: - perf_canary_degradation_success{tier="hosted",path="model"|"region"|"none"} - perf_canary_degradation_latency_ms_bucket{tier="hosted",path=...} - perf_canary_degradation_fallback_reason{tier="hosted",reason="capacity"|"health"|"policy"|"timeout"|"unknown"} (allowlist only; unknown is a single bucket) - perf_canary_degradation_expected_fallback_mismatch_total{tier="hosted",expected="model"|"region",observed=...} Mapping notes (incident taxonomy): - Sustained failures in this canary are an early warning for Routing/Fallback misbehavior vs Capacity shortage. The metric split (reason allowlist) is aligned with the router fallback_reason enums added in redwood, and is intended to support ENG-4824 taxonomy instrumentation. Rollout/ops: - Default: enabled in prod canary clusters at 1 req/min per region pair, small payload (<= 128 output tokens). - Alerting thresholds are intentionally conservative (multi-window, minimum burn) to avoid paging on transient blips; see follow-up in observability-pack. - Credentials: uses existing perf-canary service account and a dedicated routing policy ID restricted to canary traffic. Testing: - Unit tests for metadata parsing + allowlist mapping. - Integration test runs in CI against mock router endpoint. Notes: No customer-facing change. This is internal telemetry only.

title: Reduce cardinality for incident_type metric label (allowlist + sampling + unknown buckets)
preview: Context: PR #1459 introduced incident taxonomy dashboards and alert rules. During rollout review, Observability flagged that naively labeling high-volume request/edge metrics with incident_type can explode series cardinality (especially when incident-bot classification is missing/late, or when teams attempt to encode subtypes/notes). This follow-up tightens the metric labeling contract and adjusts recording rules to keep the dashboards useful without putting Prometheus/Mimir under stress. What changed: 1) Added an explicit allowlist for incident_type values used in metrics (top-level types only, no freeform). 2) Introduced two stable buckets for missing/invalid classification: incident_type="unknown" (not set) and incident_type="unclassified" (set but not in allowlist / failed validation). 3) Moved incident_type labeling off the highest-cardinality per-route/per-model series and onto lower-cardinality rollups used by the dashboards (recording rules). 4) Added a conservative sampling strategy for the remaining labeled raw series (only for error events and only for a limited set of metrics), with clear comments and defaults. 5) Updated dashboard queries and alert annotations to treat unknown/unclassified explicitly and to keep them visible (we want to drive down unknowns, not hide them). Guidance / policy baked into this repo: - Metrics must only use the top-level incident_type enum (no subtype) and must not include user/customer identifiers. - If classification is missing at emission time, emit incident_type="unknown". If a value is present but not in allowlist, normalize to incident_type="unclassified" and emit a counter for visibility. - Dashboards should break out unknown/unclassified as first-class categories so SRE can track adoption and decision-tree gaps. Rollout notes: This should be safe to deploy independently of incident-bot changes. It assumes the incident_type field exists in log/metric enrichment but tolerates it being absent. After merge, please re-run the cardinality check job in staging and confirm series counts stay within the thresholds in docs/metrics/cardinality.md. Related: ENG-4826 (metric label guardrails), ENG-4828 (dashboard build), and the earlier dashboard PR #1459.

title: Add region + hardware SKU dimension config (with fanout safeguards)
preview:
## Summary This PR adds first-class configuration for running perf-canary across multiple **regions** and **hardware SKUs**, while ensuring we don’t accidentally fan out to every region/SKU combination (and burn the overhead budget). The canary runner now supports: (1) Target selection by `region` and `hardware_sku` (optional), (2) A hard fanout guard that defaults to conservative behavior, and (3) Safer defaults for Helm/Env-based deployments. This is needed for Phase 1 rollout where we want to start in `us-east` on a single SKU, then expand gradually to `eu-west` and additional SKUs once baselines/thresholds are tuned.

## Motivation / Context Today the canary targets are effectively “global” (implicitly whatever the control plane routes to). That makes it hard to: - Attribute regressions to fleet changes (SKU-specific) vs runtime changes - Set region-specific baselines (telemetry variance differs per region) - Control cost/overhead during rollout (avoid accidental wide fanout)

This change is intentionally scoped to **config + selection + safety rails**. Baselines/threshold wiring is handled by baseline fetch logic in a separate PR.

## Design - **Target dimensions:** `region`, `hardware_sku` are added as *optional* dimensions to the canary target config. If `hardware_sku` is omitted, the canary runs “per region” and relies on the inference tier routing for SKU; if provided, we force requests to that SKU via the internal routing hint header (same mechanism used by benchmark-lab).
- **Fanout safeguards:** - New config key: `fanout_max_targets` (default: `3`) - New config key: `fanout_allowlist` (optional explicit list of `<region>:<sku>` tuples). If set, only those tuples run. - New startup-time safety check: if computed target count exceeds `fanout_max_targets`, the process exits non-zero unless `--allow-fanout-over-max` is explicitly set (intended only for controlled manual runs). - New “blast radius” log line on startup prints the exact computed targets and the derived capacity budget per target.
- **Metric labels / cardinality:** Region is now a standard label on canary-emitted metrics. Hardware SKU is only emitted when explicitly configured (to avoid accidental cardinality growth); when omitted, we emit `hardware_sku="unknown"` for aggregator stability.
- **Alert payload:** Alert webhook payload includes `region` + `hardware_sku` in the `scope` section so incident-bot threads are dedupe-able per tuple.

## Config changes - New config schema fields (YAML): - `targets.regions` (list) - `targets.hardware_skus` (list, optional) - `targets.matrix_mode` (one of: `region_only`, `region_x_sku`) - `safety.fanout_max_targets` (int) - `safety.fanout_allowlist` (list of strings `region:sku` or `region:*`) - `safety.require_explicit_region` (bool, default true)

Example (Phase 1): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1"]   matrix_mode: "region_only" safety:   fanout_max_targets: 2   require_explicit_region: true ```

Example (Phase 2 w/ SKU): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]   hardware_skus: ["h100-sxm", "a100-80gb"]   matrix_mode: "region_x_sku" safety:   fanout_allowlist: ["us-east-1:h100-sxm", "eu-west-1:h100-sxm"]   fanout_max_targets: 3 ```

## Implementation notes - Added `TargetMatrix` helper to compute the final run list. This keeps selection logic out of the request loop and makes it unit-testable. - Added request routing hints: `X-Redwood-Region` and `X-Redwood-Hardware-Sku` (only when configured). These are already supported server-side for internal callers; the perf-canary service account is restricted to internal endpoints. - Extended the runner loop to include `target_scope` in logs and metric tags.

## Tests - Unit tests for matrix expansion, allowlist filtering, max-target guard, and stable ordering (so rollouts are deterministic). - Updated config loader tests to validate schema errors (e.g., `region_x_sku` with missing `hardware_skus`). - Added a small “dry-run” mode test that prints computed targets and exits.

## Rollout plan - Merge behind conservative defaults: `require_explicit_region=true`, `fanout_max_targets=3`, `matrix_mode=region_only`. - Phase 1 (ENG-4880): enable `us-east-1` only, no SKU dimension. - Phase 2: introduce SKU dimension for `h100-sxm` only, validate baseline alignment, then expand.

## Risks / Mitigations - Risk: accidental fanout increases load/cost. Mitigation: hard guard + allowlist option + explicit override flag. - Risk: metrics cardinality blowup. Mitigation: hardware_sku label only when explicitly configured; region label is bounded and already present in most dashboards.

## Screenshots / Output (dry-run) - `--dry-run` output example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:*] target_count=1 fanout_max_targets=3` - `region_x_sku` example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:h100-sxm, eu-west-1:h100-sxm] target_count=2 fanout_allowlist=2`

## Follow-ups - Baseline store needs to key off (model, runtime_version, sku, region) once we enable SKU dimension broadly. - Confirm incident-bot dedupe keys include region+sku (separate PR in incident-bot repo).

title: Add failover summary events to incident timeline for auto-populated postmortem milestones
preview:
## Context
During the 2026-01-15 controlled regional failover (us-east → eu-west), we had to manually reconstruct the key milestones (trigger time, policy flip, drain start, rollback, steady-state) across multiple sources (routing audit logs, controller logs, Slack timeline notes). This PR integrates failover automation events into the incident timeline tooling so that postmortems can auto-populate those milestones and on-call can see a consistent view during an event.

This is part of the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade program and complements the new structured failover audit events (recommendation/execution/rollback) emitted by routing/control plane.

## What this changes
- Adds a new incident-timeline ingest adapter for `failover.audit_event` records emitted by the failover controller/policy engine.
- Normalizes audit events into timeline entries with stable, low-cardinality fields.
- Adds a computed “Failover summary” block to the incident timeline view that groups related events by `failover_id` and renders:
  - Trigger / corroboration signals
  - Decision outcome (recommended vs executed)
  - Policy flip details (source region, target region, tier/route scope)
  - Rollback reason + hold-down/anti-flap context (when present)
- Auto-creates postmortem milestone candidates (timestamps) for:
  - `failover_triggered_at`
  - `failover_executed_at`
  - `traffic_shift_started_at`
  - `traffic_shift_completed_at` (best-effort, derived from final execution event or “steady-state” marker)
  - `rollback_initiated_at` and `rollback_completed_at` (when applicable)

title: Expose incident taxonomy fields in Ops Console API (type/subtype/owner) with RBAC checks
preview:
Context: As part of the incident taxonomy + ownership mapping rollout, Console (ops-only today; CS read-only later) needs to display the incident classification consistently with incident-bot and dashboards. Today Console pulls incident summaries from the internal ops API, but the response doesn’t include incident_type/subtype or the resolved owning team, which forces folks to cross-reference Slack or postmortems and leads to mismatches during live incidents.

This PR extends the internal Ops Console API to return:
- incident_type (top-level taxonomy key)
- incident_subtype (optional; taxonomy subtype key)
- owner_team (resolved primary owner, derived from type/subtype mapping)
- owner_resolution_source ("explicit" | "mapping" | "unknown") to make it clear whether the incident commander set an owner vs it was inferred

Permissioning:
- Adds an RBAC-gated read path: callers must have ops:incidents:read to see taxonomy fields.
- For CS-facing tokens (support role), the endpoint remains accessible where it already was, but taxonomy fields are withheld unless the token has the explicit read permission.
- No write APIs are added/changed in this PR; Console remains read-only for these fields.

Compatibility / safety:
- Fields are additive and nullable. For existing incidents without classification, incident_type/subtype return null and owner_team resolves to "unassigned".
- Response field names are stable snake_case and match incident-bot naming to avoid drift.
- Feature flag: taxonomy fields are behind `ops_api_incident_taxonomy_fields` (default OFF) to allow staged rollout.

Implementation notes:
- The incident record already stores `incident_type` and `incident_subtype` (set by incident-bot); we plumb them through the service layer into the API serializer.
- Owner resolution uses the control-plane mapping client (cached) with a strict allowlist of type/subtype keys; unknown keys resolve to "unassigned" and log a structured warning.
- Added audit log entry for reads that include taxonomy fields (who, incident_id, fields_exposed=true) for compliance reviews.

Testing:
- Unit tests for serializer behavior (nulls, unknown keys, mapping hit/miss).
- RBAC tests to ensure taxonomy fields are omitted when permission is missing.
- Local manual verification using Console dev env + a seeded incident with type=availability.overload.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag (this PR)
2) Enable flag for ops-only service account in staging
3) Enable flag in prod for ops-only, then expand to CS read-only tokens once permissions model is finalized (tracked in PM-913)

Follow-ups:
- Console UI wiring + filters (DES-441)
- Incident-bot closure gating already enforces type selection for new Sev0/Sev1 incidents; this PR is just to display the selected values consistently.

Screenshots: (not included; internal endpoint + JSON diff only)

Risk: Low. Additive fields + explicit RBAC + feature flag.

Refs: PM-913 (Console surfacing), ENG-4825 (owner mapping config), INT-801 (dashboard permissions / CS visibility alignment).

title: Failover controller: rate-limit automation actions + retries to prevent oscillation under noisy signals
preview:
Context: During staging rehearsals we observed oscillation risk in the failover controller when region health inputs get noisy (partial reachability loss, intermittent 5xx spikes, and delayed capacity signals). While ADR-043 + the anti-flap timers in PR-19815 reduce flip/flop behavior, we still had two failure modes: (1) the controller repeatedly re-attempts the same action (e.g., re-apply a policy flip) when the policy engine or routing API is degraded, and (2) the controller makes too many distinct automation actions across tenants/routes in a short window, amplifying load and making rollback reasoning harder.

This PR introduces explicit rate limiting for automation actions and retries, with metrics + structured logs so on-call can see when guardrails are gating behavior.

What changed (high level): - Added an in-controller rate limiter for action execution and for retry loops. - Enforced per-scope limits (global, per-region pair, per-tenant) with token-bucket semantics and jittered refill. - Converted "retry until success" paths into bounded retries with exponential backoff and a clear terminal state (ActionDeferred vs ActionFailed). - Ensured dry-run mode is not rate-limited (we still emit the same recommendation/audit payloads), but we annotate recommendations with the expected earliest execution time if rate limits were applied. - Added audit event fields for rate-limit gating (rate_limited=true, limiter_scope, next_allowed_at) so we can reconstruct why automation did/did not act.

Key guardrails/config: - controller.rate_limits.global_actions_per_minute (default: 12) - controller.rate_limits.per_regionpair_actions_per_minute (default: 6) - controller.rate_limits.per_tenant_actions_per_hour (default: 4; enforced only when tenant_id is present) - controller.rate_limits.max_retries_per_action (default: 3) - controller.rate_limits.retry_backoff_base_ms (default: 500) - controller.rate_limits.retry_backoff_max_ms (default: 8000) - controller.rate_limits.idempotency_window_s (default: 120) to collapse duplicate "apply same desired state" attempts.

Why this is safe: - Limits are conservative and designed to prevent thundering-herd behavior when the controller is the one experiencing errors (routing API 429/5xx) or when signals flap around thresholds. - We fail "closed" for safety: when rate-limited, we do not execute policy flips; we emit an audit event and set the automation decision outcome to deferred. - We keep rollback semantics unchanged; this PR only adds gating around execution/attempt frequency.

Observability: - New metrics: failover_controller_action_rate_limited_total{scope,reason}, failover_controller_retry_scheduled_total{action_type}, failover_controller_action_next_allowed_seconds{scope}, failover_controller_action_attempts_total{result}. - Added structured log fields: limiter_scope, limiter_key, tokens_before, tokens_after, retry_attempt, retry_delay_ms.

Test plan: - Unit tests for limiter accounting, per-scope precedence (global > regionpair > tenant), and deterministic jitter bounds. - Integration-style tests with a fake routing API that returns intermittent 5xx and verifies bounded retries + correct audit emissions. - Ran controller in staging with replayed signal traces from the rehearsal window; verified we cap flips and retries and do not spam routing.

Rollout: - Behind feature flag failover_controller_rate_limits_enabled (default off). - Staging default on via the game-day config pack; prod enablement will follow the go/no-go checklist with monitoring of rate_limited_total and action latency.

Notes: This complements (not replaces) ADR-043 rollback/hold-down logic. Anti-flap timers control state transitions; rate limiting controls how aggressively we attempt execution and retry under controller-side or downstream instability.

title: Add fallback policy guardrails for enterprise protected routes
preview:
Context: As part of hot-route capacity protection, routing is now capable of using capacity signals to (a) admit/deny best-effort traffic earlier and (b) optionally fall back to compatible model variants/regions under pressure. For enterprise protected routes, we need explicit guardrails so we do not silently change model/region behavior for high-SLA workloads, and so on-call can explain decisions deterministically during escalations.

What this PR does: 1) Introduces explicit fallback policy for protected enterprise routes via route-group config + policy-engine evaluation output. Default behavior for enterprise priority is strict admission control only (no fallback) unless the route group is explicitly marked as fallback-eligible. 2) Adds separate toggles for model-variant fallback vs region fallback, and enforces compliance boundary constraints for region fallback (no cross-boundary moves). 3) Adds structured decision logging to make it auditable when fallback was considered, allowed/blocked, and why. 4) Wires routing to honor the new guardrail fields when overload protection is active, ensuring best-effort shedding cannot accidentally trigger fallback on enterprise routes.

Guardrail rules (current defaults): - /v1/chat/completions (protected enterprise): NO fallback (strict admission control). Rationale: quality/behavior drift risk + tool/function behavior consistency. - /v1/embeddings (protected enterprise): model-variant fallback allowed ONLY within a declared compatibility set (same embedding dimension + tokenizer family); region fallback disabled by default. - /v1/rerank (protected enterprise): model-variant fallback allowed within compatibility set; region fallback disabled by default. - Anything not in a protected route group: existing behavior unchanged (uses existing routing fallback behavior gates).

Operational notes: This is feature-flagged behind hot_route_protection.fallback_guardrails_enabled and is safe to roll out region-by-region. When disabled, routing behaves as before (but logs a warning if a protected route group is missing an explicit guardrail entry).

Implementation details: - Policy schema: adds fallback_policy to route groups with fields allow_model_variant_fallback, allow_region_fallback, allowed_model_compatibility_set, allowed_region_scope. - Policy evaluation output: adds fallback_guardrail verdict (allowed|blocked) and reason codes (e.g., enterprise_strict_no_fallback, compatibility_set_mismatch, cross_boundary_region_blocked). - Routing: when overload protection triggers and fallback is under consideration, routing requires fallback_guardrail=allowed for enterprise tier; otherwise it will prefer strict admission control and return an overload 429 (consistent with reason codes added in pr-27441).

Tests: - Unit tests for policy validation (missing guardrail entries, invalid compatibility set, invalid region scope). - Integration tests for protected enterprise routes ensuring: (a) chat never falls back, (b) embeddings/rerank only fall back within compatibility set, (c) cross-boundary region fallback is rejected, (d) best-effort shedding does not alter enterprise fallback behavior.

Rollout plan: - Land behind flag off by default. - Enable in us-east canary allowlist with 1-2 enterprise accounts after verifying dashboards and decision logs. - Expand to eu-west after 48h of clean canary and no support escalations.

Risk: Primary risk is misconfiguration causing unexpected strictness (more 429s) rather than silent behavior drift. We mitigate via config validation, conservative defaults, and explicit warning logs when protected route groups lack guardrail config.

References: - Linear: ENG-4924 (routing admission control), ENG-4923 (policy engine priority evaluation), PM-882 (protected routes/SLO policy). - Related PRs: pr-27427 (capacity signals), pr-27441 (429 reason codes), pr-27452 (us-east canary flags).

title: Add failover safety limits per tenant (max flips/hour + blast-radius constraints)
preview:
Context: As we enable automated regional failover + rollback, we need hard guardrails to prevent a single noisy signal (or misconfig) from causing repeated policy flips or a large blast-radius for any one tenant. This PR adds per-tenant safety limits enforced by the failover controller and surfaced via structured logs/audit events for on-call visibility.

What this changes: 1) Per-tenant action rate limits: enforce max_policy_flips_per_hour (sliding window) at tenant_id scope, with optional overrides per tenant tier (free/pro/enterprise) and per route group. 2) Blast-radius constraints: prevent automated actions that would affect more than max_regions_affected or max_models_affected in a single evaluation cycle for a tenant (e.g., avoid flipping all model routes at once). 3) Enforcement visibility: when a limit blocks an action, we emit (a) a structured enforcement log entry with evidence fields and (b) a failover audit event with reason code = SAFETY_LIMIT_* for incident timelines. 4) Safe defaults + config schema: new config keys are optional but default to conservative values when automation is enabled in prod; staging values are more permissive for rehearsals.

Implementation notes: - Limiter is evaluated after policy-engine recommendation generation and before execution; dry-run still computes recommendations but marks blocked_actions[] with the enforcement reason (so rehearsals can see what would have been blocked). - Sliding window uses Redis-backed counters where available; falls back to in-memory token bucket (best effort) when Redis is unavailable, and fails closed for execution when in prod mode (dry-run remains best effort). - Blast-radius checks operate on the set of concrete policy mutations (region weights + explicit disables) to avoid ambiguity; we count distinct target regions and distinct model identifiers impacted. - Added enums + reason codes: SAFETY_LIMIT_MAX_FLIPS_PER_HOUR, SAFETY_LIMIT_MAX_REGIONS, SAFETY_LIMIT_MAX_MODELS, SAFETY_LIMIT_REDIS_UNAVAILABLE (execution blocked).

Observability: - New logs: failover_safety_enforcement {tenant_id_hash, tenant_tier, route_group, requested_actions, blocked_actions, reason_code, flip_window_minutes, current_flip_count, max_flip_count, max_regions_affected, max_models_affected, controller_run_id}. - New metrics (low cardinality): failover_safety_block_total{reason_code,tier} and failover_safety_blocked_actions_total{reason_code,tier}. Note: tenant_id is intentionally not a label; we only log a hashed identifier for correlation.

Testing: - Unit tests for sliding window behavior and boundary cases (exactly at max, just over max). - Integration tests for a controller run that attempts multiple region flips for a single tenant; asserts only allowed subset is executed and remainder is blocked with deterministic ordering. - Added regression tests to ensure dry-run output includes blocked reasons.

Rollout: - Guardrails are enabled by default when failover automation feature flag is on in prod. - Overrides supported via config bundle for specific enterprise tenants during early rollout (requires explicit approval).

Follow-ups: - Metrics label cardinality hardening and dashboard panels tracked separately (see PR-19829 and ENG-2418).

title: Dedicated drain: add progress metrics + dashboard hooks
preview: Context: As part of the regional failover automation upgrade, we added a Dedicated pool drain workflow (PR #19822) but on-call has had limited visibility into whether a drain is making forward progress vs stuck (and how close we are to a safe cutover). This PR adds drain progress metrics emitted by the drain coordinator and wires those metrics into the existing failover signal dashboards so the game day runbook can validate drain progress in real time. What’s included: 1) New metrics for Dedicated drain lifecycle and progress, emitted from the dedicated-drain coordinator and keyed by pool_id (bounded) + region + customer_tier. 2) A periodic progress sampler that computes remaining nodes/replicas, in-flight request counts, and an ETA based on recent completion rate; sampler is explicitly best-effort and will not block drain execution. 3) An annotation event emitted when a drain starts/stops/aborts (to reduce manual timeline stitching). 4) Dashboard hooks: update the failover dashboards spec wiring (Grafana json in observability-pack is handled in PR #907; this PR provides the metric contract and internal dashboard wiring behind the same feature flag). Metric definitions: - dedicated_drain_state{region,pool_id,tier,state}: gauge (0/1) for states: requested|running|paused|completed|aborted|failed. - dedicated_drain_progress_percent{region,pool_id,tier}: gauge 0..100 (reported as float; rounded in dashboard). - dedicated_drain_remaining_nodes{region,pool_id,tier}: gauge. - dedicated_drain_inflight_requests{region,pool_id,tier}: gauge from gateway/routing counters (best-effort snapshot). - dedicated_drain_eta_seconds{region,pool_id,tier}: gauge; -1 when unknown (e.g., insufficient samples). - dedicated_drain_transition_total{region,tier,reason}: counter (pool_id intentionally excluded to keep cardinality bounded; pool_id is available via logs/audit events). Cardinality + safety: pool_id is bounded by Dedicated pools and is already an existing bounded identifier in internal metrics; we enforce a max distinct pool_id per region in the emitter (drops + logs once exceeded). We intentionally do not label by tenant_id/customer_id. For event-style visibility and audits, the drain workflow already emits audit events; this PR adds a short annotation emitter that references the audit event id. Testing: unit tests cover ETA calculation behavior (including noisy/stalled progress) and state transitions; integration test uses the staging drain simulator to ensure gauges get updated and cleared on completion/abort. Rollout: guarded by feature flag dedicated_drain_progress_metrics (default off). We’ll enable in staging during rehearsal; production enablement will be scoped to game day allowlist tenants/pools per ENG-2420 and the runbook checklist. Follow-ups: - Add an alert on “drain running with no progress > N minutes” once we have staging data (tracked in ENG-2426). - Add better correlation to incident timeline tooling (planned in PR #19844).
