title: Add incident owner mapping config to control plane (versioned + validated + audited)
preview:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program (incident taxonomy + ownership mapping), we need a single source of truth for incident owner resolution that is (1) versioned and reviewable, (2) validated to avoid typos / ambiguous matches, (3) auditable (who changed what + when), and (4) safe to roll forward/back without paging the wrong teams mid-incident. This PR introduces a control-plane managed config for mapping incident_type + subtype -> owning team metadata, plus guardrails and rollout tooling.

What this PR does: Adds a new control-plane config kind `incident_owner_mapping.v1` stored and served by the config service. The config maps {incident_type, incident_subtype} to: primary_owner_team, secondary_owner_team (optional), consulted_teams (optional), escalation_channel, and runbook_links (optional). Adds server-side validation (schema + allowlists + uniqueness + cardinality checks), audit logging on every publish, and a staged rollout mechanism with canary + automatic rollback on validation/serve errors.

Key behaviors / safeguards: 1) Uniqueness + precedence rules: exact match on (type, subtype) wins; type-only match (subtype="*") is allowed as a fallback; multiple matches are rejected at publish time. 2) Unknown handling: if a lookup misses, the API returns owner="UNRESOLVED" with a reason and a suggested next action ("pick owner manually") to avoid silently assigning the wrong team. 3) Validation: type/subtype must be in the taxonomy allowlist (pulled from `incident_taxonomy.v1` config); team ids must be in the org/team registry; escalation_channel must be a known Slack channel format; runbook_links must be https and match an allowlist of domains (Confluence + docs). 4) Rollout: publish supports `--stage=canary` (serves to 5% of lookups, based on stable hash of incident_id) then promote; rollback supports `--to-version=<n>` with a one-command revert.

API surface: Adds `GET /control-plane/v1/incident-owner/resolve?type=...&subtype=...` used by incident-bot and internal tooling. Response includes resolved owner metadata plus the mapping version and the matched rule key for auditing/debug.

Audit logging: Every publish/rollback writes an immutable audit record with: actor, request id, diff summary (added/removed/modified rules count), config version, and a sha256 of the full payload. Audit records are queryable in the existing control-plane audit UI and emitted to the central audit log stream.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation, match semantics, and unknown resolution; integration tests in `control-plane-api` verifying versioned reads + staged rollout; property-based test generating random mappings to ensure no ambiguous precedence slips through.

Migration/ops notes: This PR ships the config kind + resolver API but does not yet flip incident-bot to use it by default (that is in PR(s) tied to ENG-4822). Initial seed mapping lives in `config/seed/incident_owner_mapping.v1.json` and will be managed by SRE going forward. If this config is unavailable, callers should continue to fall back to existing static lookup tables until rollout is complete.

Follow-ups: (a) Add per-type runbook suggestion wiring in incident-bot output (tracked separately), (b) enforce required incident_type/owner on close once owner mapping is stable for 2 weeks, (c) add dashboard panel for unresolved owner lookups.

Refs: ENG-4825, ENG-4822. Related PRs: pr-28411 (add incident_type tag to alert payloads), pr-28503 (expose incident_type in ops console api).

title: Auto-suggest incident_type from symptoms (5xx/latency/streaming/capacity) with override
preview:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program "Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping" we’re making incident_type/subtype required in incident-bot. This PR adds lightweight heuristics to auto-suggest a likely incident type based on the initial symptoms so we reduce time-to-classify during live incidents while keeping the human as the source of truth.

What’s in this PR: 1) Suggestion engine: Adds a new suggestion module that generates a (type, subtype, confidence, rationale) suggestion from the incident creation context. Inputs include: incident-bot symptom selections (5xx spike, p95 latency regression, streaming disconnects/resets, capacity/fleet alerts), any attached alert payload (title + common labels), and a small set of structured hints we already capture (tier, region, affected surface: hosted/dedicated/private). The output is always constrained to the v1 taxonomy allowlist; if we cannot match, we return incident_type=Unknown with confidence=low.

2) UX changes (Slack modal + /incident create): When the modal opens, we compute a suggestion and pre-select the incident_type/subtype dropdowns only if the user hasn’t already picked a value. The UI shows a compact hint line: “Suggested: <type>/<subtype> (confidence: <high|medium|low>) — <rationale>”. A new “Use suggested type” toggle is included for cases where preselecting felt too aggressive during review; by default the dropdown is set but the hint is explicit and the user can change it. Any user selection overrides the suggestion immediately and we keep the final selected value as the incident’s incident_type.

3) Auditability: We store suggestion metadata alongside the incident record: suggested_type, suggested_subtype, suggestion_confidence, suggestion_reason_codes, and a boolean suggested_was_overridden (true if final != suggested). This lets SRE report on suggestion accuracy and helps iterate without changing the taxonomy (aligned with the goal of keeping taxonomy stable/low-cardinality).

4) Guardrails: Suggestions are based on an allowlist of patterns and structured symptom flags, not arbitrary freeform strings. Regex usage on alert titles is intentionally limited and reviewed to avoid brittle behavior and accidental cardinality explosions. If multiple heuristics match, we pick the highest-confidence category and include secondary matches in rationale (without auto-setting contributing factors; those remain manual).

Heuristic rules (v1): - 5xx spike: If symptom flag includes “5xx spike” OR alert title/labels match known gateway error alerts, suggest Availability > Elevated 5xx (confidence high). If error hints include “overload”/“queue depth”/“shed”, bias to Availability > Overload/Load shedding (confidence high). - p95 latency regression: If “p95 latency regression” selected and 5xx is not selected, suggest Latency > p95/p99 regression (confidence medium). If alert mentions “runtime scheduler stall” or “kv cache thrash” (known runtime alert names), suggest Latency > Runtime regression (confidence high). - streaming resets/disconnects: If “streaming disconnects/resets” selected OR alert title matches SSE/WebSocket reset monitors, suggest Streaming > Disconnects/resets (confidence high). - capacity/fleet alerts: If symptom flag includes “capacity event” OR alert matches fleet provisioning/insufficient capacity monitors, suggest Capacity > Fleet shortage (confidence high). - Default: Unknown (confidence low) with a short prompt to pick the closest taxonomy category manually.

Testing: - Unit tests for the suggestion engine with representative inputs (symptom-only, alert-only, mixed). - Snapshot tests for Slack modal rendering to ensure the hint line and override behavior stay stable. - Added a small golden test set pulled from recent incident titles (sanitized) to ensure we don’t regress matching behavior.

Rollout: Feature flag `incident_type_suggestions_enabled` (default on for eng-sre + oncall workspaces, off for other internal workspaces until we confirm no UI surprises). A follow-up will enable globally once we have a week of data and review accuracy/overrides.

Notes/Non-goals: This does not auto-route paging or change ownership mapping (handled by control plane + alert routing work). This does not attempt deep multi-cause classification; it’s a first-pass guess to accelerate humans.

References: ENG-4822 (require incident_type + owner), ENG-4821 (taxonomy definitions), ENG-4823 (pager routing), INT-772 (modal field edge case).

title: Failover controller: add optional approval hooks before executing automation
preview:
Context: As part of the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade, we want the controller to be able to run in production with automation enabled while still requiring an explicit human ack (on-call) before it flips routing policies for certain tiers/routes. Today the controller either executes immediately (when enabled) or runs dry-run only; there is no first-class “pending approval” state, so teams have been relying on manual toggles/feature flags during game days and incidents.

What this PR does: Adds an optional approval gate to the failover controller execution path. When configured, the controller will (1) compute the recommended actions as usual, (2) emit an audit event with status=PENDING_APPROVAL containing the evidence signals and the exact policy changes, and (3) pause execution until it receives an approval decision (approve/deny) or times out. The gate is per-environment and per-scope (Hosted API vs Dedicated), so staging remains fully automated for testing while production can require an ack.

Design notes: The controller loop is now a small state machine. We persist pending approvals in the control-plane store with a TTL so we don’t block the reconciliation loop. The controller periodically re-checks pending items and applies them only once approved and still valid under the current signals (re-validate before execute to avoid acting on stale evidence). If the approval backend is unavailable, we fail closed (do not execute) and continue emitting recommendations/audit events.

Config/behavior: - New config: failover_controller.approval.mode = {"none","required","required_for_production"} - New config: failover_controller.approval.timeout = 5m (default) - New config: failover_controller.approval.scopes = [hosted_api,dedicated] (default: hosted_api only) - New config: failover_controller.approval.require_revalidation = true (default)

Interfaces: - Adds ApprovalProvider interface with NoopApprovalProvider and ControlPlaneApprovalProvider (decision written via internal API/CLI). This PR does not hard-depend on Slack/incident-bot; it exposes a stable API for Devon’s incident-bot follow-up to post buttons/commands.

Observability: - Metrics: failover_approval_pending_total, failover_approval_decision_total{decision=approve|deny|timeout}, failover_approval_wait_seconds (histogram) - Logs: structured log lines include approval_id, action_digest, tenant_tier, route_id, reason - Audit events: PENDING_APPROVAL, APPROVED, DENIED, TIMED_OUT

Testing: - Unit tests for state transitions and re-validation behavior - Integration test that simulates: recommendation -> pending -> approve -> execute (and a stale-signal reject) - Adds a small fake approval backend used by controller integration tests

Rollout: - Feature-flagged behind failover_controller.approval.enabled - Default mode remains “none” so existing staging/game-day configs are unaffected until explicitly enabled

Notes for on-call: There is a new internal endpoint to approve/deny a pending approval (see below). Runbook updates will be a separate doc-focused PR (ENG-2423) once the incident-bot UX lands.

Endpoints: - POST /internal/failover/approvals/{approval_id}: {decision: approve|deny, actor: <user>} - GET /internal/failover/approvals?state=pending

Screenshots: N/A (backend only)

Follow-ups: - incident-bot integration for posting pending approvals + one-click approve/deny - console banner behavior during “pending approval” state - add per-tenant safety limits to approval scope (max pending per tenant)

Refs: ADR-043 (rollback/anti-flap) and ongoing discussion in #eng-sre re: manual approval vs full automation in prod.

Config/behavior: - New config: failover_controller.approval.mode = {"none","required","required_for_production"} - New config: failover_controller.approval.timeout = 5m (default) - New config: failover_controller.approval.scopes = [hosted_api,dedicated] (default: hosted_api only) - New config: failover_controller.approval.require_revalidation = true (default)

Interfaces: - Adds ApprovalProvider interface with NoopApprovalProvider and ControlPlaneApprovalProvider (decision written via internal API/CLI). This PR does not hard-depend on Slack/incident-bot; it exposes a stable API for Devon’s incident-bot follow-up to post buttons/commands.

Observability: - Metrics: failover_approval_pending_total, failover_approval_decision_total{decision=approve|deny|timeout}, failover_approval_wait_seconds (histogram) - Logs: structured log lines include approval_id, action_digest, tenant_tier, route_id, reason - Audit events: PENDING_APPROVAL, APPROVED, DENIED, TIMED_OUT

Testing: - Unit tests for state transitions and re-validation behavior - Integration test that simulates: recommendation -> pending -> approve -> execute (and a stale-signal reject) - Adds a small fake approval backend used by controller integration tests

Rollout: - Feature-flagged behind failover_controller.approval.enabled - Default mode remains “none” so existing staging/game-day configs are unaffected until explicitly enabled

Notes for on-call: There is a new internal endpoint to approve/deny a pending approval (see below). Runbook updates will be a separate doc-focused PR (ENG-2423) once the incident-bot UX lands.

Endpoints: - POST /internal/failover/approvals/{approval_id}: {decision: approve|deny, actor: <user>} - GET /internal/failover/approvals?state=pending

Screenshots: N/A (backend only)

Follow-ups: - incident-bot integration for posting pending approvals + one-click approve/deny - console banner behavior during “pending approval” state - add per-tenant safety limits to approval scope (max pending per tenant)

Refs: ADR-043 (rollback/anti-flap) and ongoing discussion in #eng-sre re: manual approval vs full automation in prod.

title: Hotfix: prevent failover controller NPE when a signal stream is missing
preview:
Context: During staging rehearsal for regional failover automation, the failover controller crashed with a nil pointer dereference when one of the signal streams (capacity/headroom) was absent for a subset of regions/routes. This can happen during partial outages of the signal publisher, misconfigured routing configs in staging, or when a region is newly added and the signal pipeline hasn’t started publishing yet. The controller should degrade safely (no action + clear evidence) rather than crash-loop.

What changed: 1) Make signal reads total: convert all signal fetch paths in the controller to return an explicit tri-state (present/absent/stale) instead of a nil map/struct. Missing signals no longer cause an NPE. 2) Deterministic behavior on missing inputs: if a signal required for corroboration (per ADR-042) is missing, the controller marks the evaluation as `insufficient_evidence` and produces a no-op plan; we do not flip policies based on partial evidence. If the missing signal is non-authoritative for the decision path, we proceed but record `missing_signal_sources` on the evaluation log/audit event. 3) Observability: add `failover_controller_missing_signal_total{signal=<name>,region=<region>}` counter and emit a structured log + audit event annotation when a required stream is missing for >2 evaluation intervals. 4) Safety: add a short circuit to avoid writing partial plans when evaluation errors occur; controller returns a stable empty plan and increments `failover_controller_eval_errors_total`.

Tests: Adds a regression test that reproduces the panic (signal store returns absent for a configured signal). Adds table-driven tests for the new evaluation contract (required vs optional signals) and verifies the controller produces a no-op plan and emits the missing-signal metric/log.

Impact/Risk: Low functional risk (hotfix + behavior only affects missing-signal cases). This reduces the chance of controller crash-loop during impairment, and avoids acting on incomplete evidence.

Rollout: Merge to main and deploy to staging immediately. After 30 minutes of stable evaluations (no panic, expected metric increments only), promote to production via standard controller rollout (helm chart values unchanged; behavior is code-only). On-call notes included below.

title: Dedicated: add internal pool drain endpoint + guarded workflow for regional failover
preview:
Context: As part of the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade (ENG-2411), we need a safe, deterministic way to drain Dedicated GPU pools during regional impairment and controlled failovers. Today, draining is a manual sequence across fleet tooling + routing toggles, which is slow and error-prone during an incident. This PR adds an internal endpoint and workflow to request/observe a drain, with explicit safety gates and auditability.

What’s in this PR: 1) New internal control-plane endpoint: POST /internal/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/drain (and GET /internal/dedicated/pools/{pool_id}/drain/{drain_id}) that initiates a drain request and returns a drain_id for tracking. Requests are idempotent via Idempotency-Key and constrained by RBAC (internal operator + automation controller only). 2) Drain workflow/state machine: QUEUED -> VALIDATING -> DRAINING -> WAITING_FOR_ZERO -> COMPLETE (or FAILED/CANCELED). The controller will: (a) validate pool eligibility (tier, region, model family constraints), (b) coordinate with routing to stop new allocations to the pool, (c) gradually reduce schedulable capacity (rate-limited, configurable), (d) wait for active sessions/in-flight requests to fall below thresholds, and (e) mark drain complete only after a stable window. 3) Safety gates: max concurrent drains per region, max drain rate, minimum regional headroom requirement (signals from fleet capacity service), and explicit “do not drain” for pools with pinned single-region residency. For production, execution is behind the feature flag dedicated.drain_endpoint.enabled and also supports dry-run mode via ?mode=dry_run to compute the plan + validations without mutating state. 4) Observability: new metrics dedicated_drain_requests_total, dedicated_drain_state (gauge by state), dedicated_drain_progress (0-1), dedicated_pool_active_sessions, and structured audit events emitted on request/transition/complete/fail (used by incident-bot summaries and game day timelines).

Why this approach: Drain is treated as a first-class operation with an auditable state machine, rather than a pile of one-off scripts. The endpoint is intentionally internal-only and designed for integration with the failover controller; on-call can also invoke it during manual mitigation with clear rollback/cancel behavior.

Rollout plan: 1) Staging: enable flag globally; run soak tests + simulated drains during rehearsals. 2) Production: enable for a small allowlist of test tenants/pools used in controlled failovers; require approval hooks at the controller layer (separate PR). 3) Expand to more Dedicated tiers after game day success criteria are met.

Risks + mitigations: Main risk is draining too aggressively and causing customer-visible errors. Mitigations: validation gates require headroom, rate limits on capacity reduction, and routing coordination to stop new placements first. The workflow can be canceled (POST .../cancel) which halts further drain steps and restores target schedulable capacity to the last known good value.

Testing: Unit tests cover state transitions, idempotency, and validation logic; integration tests run against a fake fleet backend; e2e staging test demonstrates drain completes with active sessions decreasing to zero. Added docs in code comments for on-call usage; runbook updates tracked in ENG-2423.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): wiring this into automated regional failover workflows (controller calls drain endpoint), dashboards panel additions, and tightening residency policy validation based on enterprise contracts (tracked separately).

title: Add integration tests for tiered quota + priority routing (quota windows, fallback vs shedding, concurrent load)
preview:
## Summary
Adds a focused integration test suite validating the new hot-route capacity protection behavior end-to-end across: API Gateway (tier-aware rate limiting + quota headers), Policy Engine (priority class + deterministic decisions), and Routing (admission control + fallback guardrails).

This PR covers three high-risk areas called out in the rollout plan:
1) quota windows behave correctly and are observable (429 semantics/headers are stable)
2) priority routing behavior is correct under pressure (enterprise budget preserved; best-effort throttled first)
3) correctness under concurrency (no racy accounting; decisions stay deterministic under load)

## Why
We had good unit coverage per component, but gaps showed up during canary when multiple signals interact (quota vs overload vs fallback). We need a single harness that spins up gateway+routing+policy with predictable time + predictable capacity signals and asserts customer-visible behavior.

Related:
- ENG-4922 (gateway tier-aware rate limiter)
- ENG-4923 (policy schema: priority classes + route budgets)
- ENG-4924 (routing admission control using capacity signals)
- SUP-18421 (enterprise perceived 429 spike; need to validate reason codes + headers)
- SUP-18462 (streaming clients; ensure consistent termination reason surfaced when shed)

## What’s included
### New test harness
- `testkit/hotroute/` brings up gateway/routing/policy-engine with:
  - ephemeral ports
  - in-memory config provider (route groups + tier defaults)
  - fake clock (no `time.Sleep()` based quota tests)
  - capacity-signal stub (queue depth + scheduler lag) with stepwise scenarios
  - deterministic request IDs + decision IDs for assertions

### Integration test scenarios
1) **Quota windows**
   - validates rolling window accounting (per-minute + per-hour) using fake clock advances
   - asserts 429 payload + headers are stable:
     - `x-redwood-throttle-reason: quota_exhausted`
     - `retry-after` and `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` present and consistent
     - `x-redwood-tier` and `x-redwood-route-group` set for supportability
   - verifies that quota 429s do *not* flip overload counters and that gateway emits the correct metric dimension (`reason=quota_exhausted`)

2) **Priority fallback vs shedding**
   - with simulated fleet pressure (queue depth above threshold):
     - best-effort traffic receives 429 with `x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_shed` first
     - enterprise tier continues to be admitted up to its route budget
   - fallback guardrail coverage:
     - routes marked `fallback_allowed=false` are admission-controlled only (no silent model change)
     - routes marked `fallback_allowed=true` may fallback to an allowed compatible model when capacity is constrained
   - validates that fallback decisions are logged deterministically (policy decision log contains `decision=FALLBACK` and the selected target)

3) **Concurrent load correctness**
   - `N=200` concurrent requests with mixed tiers and mixed route groups
   - asserts:
     - no negative counters / no over-admission beyond configured budgets
     - quota window accounting is stable under contention
     - policy decision cache keys do not leak tier/tenant across requests
   - runs with race detection in CI (`-race`) for the shared in-process test fixtures

### CI wiring
- adds a dedicated job `integration-hot-route-protection` to run only these tests on PRs touching gateway/routing/policy paths
- nightly run still executes the full integration suite; this job is a fast subset (~4–6 min)

## Not included
- Full multi-tenant fairness scheduling rewrite (out of scope)
- Billing changes (we only validate headers/metadata presence; we do not assert metering pipeline)

## How to run locally
- `just test-integration hotroute`
- or `go test ./integration/hotroute -run TestHotRoute -count=1`

## Risk
Low. Test-only changes + CI wiring. Minor risk of flake due to startup timing; mitigated by explicit health checks and fake clock for time-based assertions.

## Screenshots / artifacts
- CI run: green (see checks below)
- Example failing assertion output includes full 429 payload + headers to help Support/SRE debug regressions.

## Checklist
- [x] Validates 429 reason codes and retry headers
- [x] Validates tier routing under synthetic pressure
- [x] Validates fallback guardrails
- [x] Concurrency coverage
- [x] Added docs links in test README for on-call reference

---

### Notes on customer-visible semantics asserted by these tests
- `quota_exhausted` is strictly a quota decision (predictable, window-based)
- `overload_shed` is strictly an overload/admission decision (capacity-signal driven)
- enterprise tier may still see 429s when enterprise budget is exceeded; reason code differs (`enterprise_budget_exhausted`) and includes decision context headers

(These align with the new documentation in redwood-docs; see redwood-docs PR-2217 for public wording.)

title: Hotfix: fix metric cardinality regression in overload events
preview: Context: During the hot-route capacity protection rollout we introduced new overload event metrics (shed/admission_control) that were intended to be low-cardinality (route_group, tier, region, reason). In the last merge window we accidentally started emitting per-request / per-tenant labels on the overload event counters due to a tag plumbing change in the gateway -> telemetry bridge. This caused a rapid increase in series count in Prometheus and intermittent ingestion backpressure, and it also made the SRE dashboards noisy/slow to load. What changed: (1) Add a strict label allowlist for overload event metrics in the telemetry emitter and drop/ignore all non-allowed labels (e.g., tenant_id, request_id, trace_id, api_key_id, user_agent, model_revision). (2) Split the old overloaded "overload_event" into two metrics: redwood_overload_shed_total and redwood_overload_admission_denied_total with a stable label set {route_group, tier, region, reason}. (3) Add a lightweight counter redwood_overload_labels_dropped_total{metric_name,label_key} to make future regressions visible without paging the fleet. (4) Update dashboard queries to use the new metrics and remove the accidental group-by on tenant_id. Notes: This is intentionally conservative and enforces invariants close to the emitter to avoid future regressions from upstream tag propagation. This does not change overload behavior itself; only metric emission. Validation: Verified in staging by replaying overload simulation traffic and confirming series count stays flat; production canary (us-east) shows series count returning to baseline within ~30 minutes after rollout. Checklist: [x] Unit tests for allowlist behavior + label drop accounting [x] Update dashboards/recording rules in monorepo bundle [x] Backwards-compat shim: keep the old metric name as an alias for 7 days (deprecated) to avoid alert breakage during rollout [x] Added runbook note: "If series count spikes, check labels_dropped_total". Rollout plan: Merge + deploy with standard gateway release train; no flag required. Monitoring: prometheus_remote_write_queue, series_count by job=telemetry, and the hot-route protection dashboards (ENG-4926).

title: Post overload-protection activations to #incidents with dashboards + runbook links
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new incident-bot event handler for Hot-route Capacity Protection activation events emitted by routing/gateway. When protection enters an active state (or exits), incident-bot posts a structured Slack message to `#incidents` with:
- route_group + route (when available), region, tier class, and activation type
- primary reason code(s) (quota vs overload) and protection mode (admission control vs shedding)
- current/peak metrics snapshot (shed_rate, 429_rate, p95 latency, queue depth where present)
- links to the SRE dashboard + runbook section
- a dedupe key to avoid paging storms during flappy thresholds

This is intended to reduce time-to-diagnosis during overload events and make it obvious when best-effort is being throttled to preserve enterprise SLOs.

## Context
Hot-route capacity protection is rolling out behind flags (see ENG-4927). During the us-east canary we observed multiple on-call threads asking “are we currently shedding?” and “which runbook section applies?”. A lightweight, deterministic bot post was the fastest way to improve shared situational awareness.

## Behavior
### When we post
- On transition into ACTIVE (per region + route_group + tier_class)
- On transition out of ACTIVE (recovery)
- Optional periodic reminder every 20 minutes while ACTIVE if shed_rate remains above threshold (disabled by default; behind `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED`)

### Dedupe / rate limiting
- Dedupe key: `(region, route_group, tier_class, mode)`
- Minimum repeat interval: 10 minutes (configurable)
- If the source emits frequent toggles, we collapse and post an update message (thread reply) instead of new top-level posts.

### Message format (example)
Top-level post:
- Title: `Overload protection ACTIVE: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise` 
- Fields:
  - Mode: `admission_control + shedding`
  - Reason: `OVERLOAD_QUEUE_DEPTH` (plus any secondary reason codes)
  - Shed rate (best-effort): `12.4% (5m)`
  - 429 rate (enterprise): `0.3% (5m)`
  - p95 latency (enterprise): `820ms (5m)`
- Links:
  - Dashboard: `Hot Route Protection / us-east / chat_completions`
  - Runbook: `Runbook: Overload protection and admission control (Diagnose)`
  - Debug endpoint (internal): `Admission decision inspector` (only shown if `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK=true`)

Recovery post:
- Title: `Overload protection RECOVERED: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise`
- Includes duration in active state and a small “before/after” snapshot.

## Implementation notes
- New event type: `overload_protection.activation.v1`
- Source payload is treated as untrusted input; all fields are schema-validated and truncated to keep Slack payload small.
- We intentionally avoid embedding high-cardinality identifiers (tenant_id, request_id) in the Slack message. The dashboard link includes filters but not tenant scoping.
- Confluence + dashboard links are generated from a simple mapping keyed by `route_group` and `region`.

## Config
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_POSTS_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_CHANNEL` (default: #incidents)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_DEDUPE_MINUTES` (default: 10)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` (default: false)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - schema validation + truncation
  - dedupe + threading behavior
  - link generation
  - ACTIVE -> ACTIVE updates vs ACTIVE -> RECOVERED transitions
- Manual test against Slack sandbox workspace using the replay CLI.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable only in staging for 48h; validate dedupe + message formatting
3) Canary enable in us-east for protected routes only; verify no spam during expected best-effort shedding

text:
## Summary
This PR adds a new incident-bot event handler for Hot-route Capacity Protection activation events emitted by routing/gateway. When protection enters an active state (or exits), incident-bot posts a structured Slack message to `#incidents` with:
- route_group + route (when available), region, tier class, and activation type
- primary reason code(s) (quota vs overload) and protection mode (admission control vs shedding)
- current/peak metrics snapshot (shed_rate, 429_rate, p95 latency, queue depth where present)
- links to the SRE dashboard + runbook section
- a dedupe key to avoid paging storms during flappy thresholds

This is intended to reduce time-to-diagnosis during overload events and make it obvious when best-effort is being throttled to preserve enterprise SLOs.

## Context
Hot-route capacity protection is rolling out behind flags (see ENG-4927). During the us-east canary we observed multiple on-call threads asking “are we currently shedding?” and “which runbook section applies?”. A lightweight, deterministic bot post was the fastest way to improve shared situational awareness.

## Behavior
### When we post
- On transition into ACTIVE (per region + route_group + tier_class)
- On transition out of ACTIVE (recovery)
- Optional periodic reminder every 20 minutes while ACTIVE if shed_rate remains above threshold (disabled by default; behind `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED`)

### Dedupe / rate limiting
- Dedupe key: `(region, route_group, tier_class, mode)`
- Minimum repeat interval: 10 minutes (configurable)
- If the source emits frequent toggles, we collapse and post an update message (thread reply) instead of new top-level posts.

### Message format (example)
Top-level post:
- Title: `Overload protection ACTIVE: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise` 
- Fields:
  - Mode: `admission_control + shedding`
  - Reason: `OVERLOAD_QUEUE_DEPTH` (plus any secondary reason codes)
  - Shed rate (best-effort): `12.4% (5m)`
  - 429 rate (enterprise): `0.3% (5m)`
  - p95 latency (enterprise): `820ms (5m)`
- Links:
  - Dashboard: `Hot Route Protection / us-east / chat_completions`
  - Runbook: `Runbook: Overload protection and admission control (Diagnose)`
  - Debug endpoint (internal): `Admission decision inspector` (only shown if `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK=true`)

Recovery post:
- Title: `Overload protection RECOVERED: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise`
- Includes duration in active state and a small “before/after” snapshot.

## Implementation notes
- New event type: `overload_protection.activation.v1`
- Source payload is treated as untrusted input; all fields are schema-validated and truncated to keep Slack payload small.
- We intentionally avoid embedding high-cardinality identifiers (tenant_id, request_id) in the Slack message. The dashboard link includes filters but not tenant scoping.
- Confluence + dashboard links are generated from a simple mapping keyed by `route_group` and `region`.

## Config
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_POSTS_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_CHANNEL` (default: #incidents)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_DEDUPE_MINUTES` (default: 10)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` (default: false)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - schema validation + truncation
  - dedupe + threading behavior
  - link generation
  - ACTIVE -> ACTIVE updates vs ACTIVE -> RECOVERED transitions
- Manual test against Slack sandbox workspace using the replay CLI.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable only in staging for 48h; validate dedupe + message formatting
3) Canary enable in us-east for protected routes only; verify no spam during expected best-effort shedding
4) Expand to other regions post GA enablement window

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4927
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Runbook%3A+Overload+protection+and+admission+control
- Dashboard spec: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Dashboard+spec%3A+Hot-route+protection
- Related PRs:
  - redwood/pr-27441-add-overload-reason-codes-to-429-responses
  - observability-pack/pr-1832-add-hot-route-protection-dashboards

- [x] Includes feature flag default-off
- [x] Includes dedupe protections
- [x] Includes tests
- [x] No high-cardinality fields in message text
- [x] Docs link points to correct runbook section

title: Require incident_type/subtype on create; auto-resolve owner + escalation channel
preview:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”, we’re making incident_type a required field at incident creation time, and we want the bot to resolve an owner team consistently (single primary owner) to reduce time-to-engage and improve reporting quality.

This PR updates the Slack modal + slash command flow to require incident_type and incident_subtype (from the v1 allowlist), then performs owner resolution (type/subtype -> primary owner + escalation channel) and posts the result into the incident channel. The goal is: (1) classification is required and auditable, (2) owner is consistently derived from a versioned mapping, and (3) the IC gets an immediate “who owns this” + “where to escalate” suggestion.

User-facing changes:
- /incident create now blocks submission until incident_type is selected.
- incident_subtype is required when the type has subtypes; otherwise the field is hidden.
- After incident is created, bot posts a short summary: incident_type/subtype, resolved owner team, escalation channel, and a reminder that IC can override classification if needed.
- If mapping is missing (e.g., new subtype), we fall back to SRE on-call owner and label classification as “needs-mapping”; bot also files a lightweight internal warning event.

Implementation notes:
- Added TaxonomyAllowlist (static JSON in-repo for now) with strict validation + display labels.
- Added OwnerResolver that consults the mapping (fetched via config service; local fallback for dev/test).
- Added “override owner” admin-only action in the incident channel (logs audit event; does not change the canonical mapping).
- Added structured fields to incident record: incident_type, incident_subtype, owner_team, escalation_channel, owner_resolution_source.

Out of scope:
- Auto-suggest incident_type from symptoms (tracked separately).
- Retrofitting historical incidents (backfill job lives in redwood/observability).

Rollout:
- Behind feature flag: incident_taxonomy_required_fields.
- Enabled for eng-sre workspace first; then global after 1 week if no issues.

Checklist:
- [x] Slack modal validation for required fields
- [x] Owner resolution + fallback behavior
- [x] Audit/event logging for overrides
- [x] Unit tests for allowlist parsing + resolver
- [x] E2E test for create flow
- [x] Docs snippet in /help output

Screenshots/GIF: attached in PR (Slack modal with incident_type dropdown + subtype conditional).
